title: GrowMechanics: Designing Non-Deterministic Mechanical Behavior through Biological Growth
authors: Madalina Nicolae, Lining Yao
publish: Proceedings of the 2026 Designing Interactive Systems Conference
abstract: None

title: Method of Autonomous Quality Evaluation for Space Remote Sensing Processing Data Based on Transfer Learning
authors: Zhigang Wu, Luosi Liu, Haochen He
publish: 2025 IEEE 11th International Conference on Control Science and Systems Engineering (ICCSSE)
abstract: Intelligent processing techniques for space remote sensing data are currently facing technical bottlenecks, leading to issues such as blurred and distorted images, low confidence levels in detection and recognition, and difficulties in direct use of the processed data. To address these challenges, incorporating quality evaluation information into space remote sensing processing data can enable users to refer to evaluation results and adopt flexible data usage strategies, thereby significantly improving the effectiveness and flexibility of utilizing such data. To enhance the accuracy and timeliness of autonomous quality evaluation for space remote sensing processing data, there is an urgent need to develop innovative methods. This study proposes a method of autonomous quality evaluation for space remote sensing processing data based on transfer learning. The method employs a multi-feature fusion approach to perform comprehensive quality evaluations without reference data, generating a sample database for data quality. Experimental results demonstrate that this method can provide quality composite scores and classification outcomes, offering technical support for the creation of the data quality sample library. Furthermore, an intelligent quality evaluation method based on transfer learning is used to conduct supervised quality assessments. Experimental results show that the transfer learning effects of ResNet50, VGG16, and GoogLeNet models are as expected. Among these, the ResNet50 and VGG16 models achieve higher accuracy in intelligent evaluations compared to the GoogLeNet model. Additionally, the classification results from the ResNet50 model exhibit the best consistency with the comprehensive evaluation results. The study concludes that the proposed method of autonomous quality evaluation for space remote sensing processing data based on transfer learning has promising applications in intelligent processing of space remote sensing data and provides an effective solution for practical applications.

title: Simulation and Analysis of Starlight Transmission Through Martian Dust Aerosols
authors: Hu Kai, Zheng Xunjiang, Sun Shuodong, Zhang Xuwei, Jiang Lihui, Dai Feng
publish: 2025 IEEE 11th International Conference on Control Science and Systems Engineering (ICCSSE)
abstract: Although the Martian atmosphere is thin, starlight is attenuated by periodic dust storms, which makes astronomical navigation techniques that depend on stellar measurements difficult to employ. This study used the optical properties of Martian dust to build a dust microparticle model and a spatiotemporal evolution model for regional dust storms in order to evaluate the effect of dust storms on starlight transmittance through the Martian atmosphere. The model simulated the distribution of dust particles, computed optical thickness over the visible to near-infrared spectrum, and used the Beer-Lambert law to examine changes in starlight transmittance under various dust concentrations, observation wavelengths, and zenith angles. Dust storms dramatically increase optical thickness, resulting in decreased transmission, according to numerical simulations. The attenuation shows a positive correlation with wavelength and a negative correlation with zenith angle and dust content. Transmittance at 400 nm and 1000 nm dropped from $87.4 \%$ and $89.8 \%$ during calm periods to $62.7 \%$ and $68.4 \%$, respectively, during peak dust storm intensity at an observation zenith angle of 0. Transmittance further dropped to $36.3 \%$ and $52.5 \%$ at a zenith angle of 60°. Additionally, transmission decreased more at short wavelengths than at long wavelengths. These findings suggest that during dust storms, attenuation is more noticeable for lowelevation stars and short-wavelength photons. For upcoming Mars exploration missions, the results offer theoretical guidance for developing observation plans, choosing the best wavelength bands, and building astronomical navigation systems.

title: A Hybrid VGG19–ResNet50 Feature Fusion Framework with Imbalance-Aware Learning for Robust Brain Tumor Classification
authors: SivaKumar Depuru, Mokkala Kiran Moni, Suvarna Yashaswini, Dudekula Pedda Madarsa, R Neeradh Kumar Reddy, P C Jaswanth
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Intelligent Communication Technologies and Virtual Mobile Networks (ICICV)
abstract: MRI scans continue to be a significant challenge in the accurate classification of brain tumors as high inter-class similarity, heterogeneity of tumors and grossly unbalanced classes of the complete set of classes make such classification difficult resulting in biased predictions in the majority classes. In order to overcome these shortcomings, this research proposes a hybrid deep learning architecture that combines VGG19 and ResNet50 by implementing feature-level fusion and learning on imbalanced data. The architecture integrates hierarchical spatial feature extraction of VGG19 with residual semantic learning of ResNet50 to get a more detailed representation of tumors. During training, focal loss and synthetic augmentation methods are used to reduce the imbalance in data. This model was tested on the BraTS2020 dataset with 3, 000 MRI slices of four tumor types. The experimental results show an overall accuracy of 88, macro F1-score of 85.5 and average AUC of 0.92 is more than standalone VGG19 (83.4% accuracy) and ResNet50 (84.7% accuracy). The results suggest that feature fusion in the forms of a hybrid is much more effective in enhancing the discriminative performance and reliability in the multi-class brain tumor classification.

title: Robust Fault-Tolerant Control for Dual-Drone Slung Load Systems Under Rotor Damage
authors: Xiaoyu Yi, Ziya Jiang, Yu Tian, Xiaolong Zhu
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Electrical Engineering, Control and Robotics (EECR)
abstract: This paper proposes an integrated hierarchical fault-tolerant control framework to address the control challenges of a dual-drone cooperative slung load system under partial rotor failure. The primary contributions of this work are twofold: 1) A hierarchical robust controller with a combined inner-outer loop structure is constructed to achieve precise position tracking and load swing suppression; 2) A finite-time disturbance observer is designed for each drone to actively compensate for the lumped disturbances caused by the fault. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed framework effectively estimates and compensates for fault-induced disturbances, ensuring system stability and minimizing payload oscillation even under severe rotor damage conditions, thereby verifying the outstanding contribution of the proposed framework in improving system robustness.

title: Representation Geometry Preservation for Robust Neural Network Pruning
authors: Anastasia S. Raskopina, Tatiana M. Tatarnikova, Ren Jianan
publish: 2026 Wave Electronics and its Application in Information and Telecommunication Systems (WECONF)
abstract: Structural pruning is widely used to reduce the computational complexity of deep neural networks. However, most existing approaches rely on local importance criteria and do not explicitly analyze the internal geometry of learned representations. In this work, structured pruning is investigated from the perspective of representation geometry preservation. The study focuses on spectral characteristics of class similarity matrices and statistical properties of channel activations. An analysis of spectral entropy and condition number under different pruning levels demonstrates that structural simplification does not lead to spectral collapse or numerical instability of representations. The results show that pruning guided by activation variance preserves the global organization of class relationships while maintaining classification performance.

title: Aerial Pursuit: Multi-Agent Swarm for Predator–Prey Games
authors: Wu Zining, Chen Haoran, Zhao Jinqiu, Peng Yifeng, Sutthiphong Srigrarom, Boo Cheong Khoo
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Electrical Engineering, Control and Robotics (EECR)
abstract: This paper addresses the cold-start and convergence challenges of multi-agent adversarial reinforcement learning in physics-rich environments. The UAVs are modeled as predator-prey agents inspired by eagle-hen interactions. We propose a curriculum-based two-stage training scheme: Stage 1 trains the hens against a heuristic eagle, and Stage 2 freezes the learned hen policy while training the eagle. Using Box2D physics and Gymnasium interfaces, we build a reproducible engineering pipeline based on Stable-Baselines3 PPO, parallel sampling, and TensorBoard logging. Our main contributions include environment modeling (world construction, chain joints, boundary handling, and bounce dynamics), observation and reward design, parallel training with evaluation callbacks, and behavior visualization with metric analysis. The results show improved defensive behavior by the hens in Stage 1, reflected in increasing mean returns, explained variance approaching 1, and decreasing entropy loss. In Stage 2, the eagle learns effective approach and capture behaviors, producing positive potential-based rewards and sparse positive returns upon capture while maintaining smooth overall training curves. Remaining limitations include the empirical tuning of reward weights and physical parameters. Future work will explore joint fine-tuning, self-play, and more complex scenarios.

title: Wide-Aperture Sensor for Atmospheric and Space Optical Quantum Communication Systems
authors: D. D. Chizhin, V. V. Toporovsky, A. Yu. Epifanov, I. V. Galaktionov, N. V. Pchelkina
publish: 2026 Wave Electronics and its Application in Information and Telecommunication Systems (WECONF)
abstract: This paper presents a theoretical investigation and experimental analysis of wavefront sensors based on the ShackHartmann configuration and the Talbot effect. A comparative analysis of their characteristics indicates that the Talbot effectbased wavefront sensor is comparable to the Shack-Hartmann scheme in terms of input resolution, measurement frequency, and accuracy. The experiment demonstrates the feasibility of employing the Talbot effect-based wavefront sensor in adaptive optics systems for atmospheric and space optical communication channels, including those utilizing quantum key distribution technology.

title: Early Detection of Brain Tumors from MRI Images using Machine Learning Models
authors: Bejin Benjamin B, Sherin Eliyas
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Intelligent Communication Technologies and Virtual Mobile Networks (ICICV)
abstract: Brain tumors are serious neurological disorders that must be diagnosed quickly and accurately for patients to have better chances of survival and improved clinical outcomes. Because of the lack of side effects and the good detail/clarity offered by MRIs, MRIs are the primary way to identify brain tumors. However, MRIs must be interpreted by physicians, and any errors made by the physician require accurate interpretation. As a solution to these problems, this research proposes an automated way to determine the presence of a brain tumor to classify the different outcomes using MRI images. To achieve this, we will utilize advanced deep learning and machine learning technologies to implement a deep learning system. Four distinct groups are employed by the suggested strategy for organising brain magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) scans: meningioma, pituitary tumor, glioma, and no tumor. MRI images used in this study are obtained from the Kaggle Brain Tumor MRI dataset, along with other publicly available benchmark datasets. Prior to classification, methods for preparing images such as Normalisation and resizing, and noise reduction are applied to enhance image quality and improve model performance. Advanced deep learning models, including Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) and transfer learning architectures such as VGG16 and ResNet, are employed for feature extraction and classification. Additionally, Regular machine learning classifiers, such as RF (random forest) and supportive vector machine (SVM), are implemented for comparative analysis.

title: Dynamic Modeling and Analysis of Asteroid Flexible Brush Sampling
authors: Taotao Hu, Fan Guo, He Zhang
publish: 2025 IEEE 11th International Conference on Control Science and Systems Engineering (ICCSSE)
abstract: Sampling techniques for asteroid regolith represent a critical challenge in deep-space exploration missions. Flexible brush sampling technology has garnered significant attention due to its high efficiency and low disturbance characteristics. This paper systematically investigates the dynamic response and sampling performance of flexible sampling mechanisms in microgravity environments using a coupled simulation method integrating particle dynamics and flexible multibody dynamics. The reliability of the coupled model is validated through multisource verification using drop tower experimental data and literature results. Based on the validated model, the study delves into the impact of key parameters-including brush elastic modulus, damping ratio, mechanism feed rate, and rotational speed-on sampling efficiency. Results indicate that increasing the brush’s elastic modulus and rotational speed, while reducing the feed rate, effectively enhances sampling performance. Furthermore, multidimensional comparisons elucidate the differing sampling mechanisms between flexible and rigid brush mechanisms: the flexible brush periodically stores and releases strain energy, converting collision-dissipated energy from the rigid mechanism into kinetic energy for particle transport.

title: Designing of Mobile-Assisted Foreign Language Learning System
authors: Natalia Alexandrovna Kopylova, Shamil Alievich Otsokov, Marina Anatolievna Slepneva
publish: 2026 8th International Conference on Information Technologies in Engineering Education (Inforino)
abstract: The integration of rapidly evolving digital technologies is transforming our life, including education, thus necessitating new pedagogical approaches and reviewing the methodology of teaching disciplines. The aim of this study was to design an innovative approach – a mobile-assisted foreign language learning system partly built on artificial intelligence and deployed via a popular messenger platform. The authors present the architecture of one of the options for teaching grammar in the language messenger channel using the example of the English tenses system in the form of a goal tree, the idea of which is used to represent knowledge as a hierarchy of tasks or concepts necessary for mastering. The application developed to improve E-learning in a foreign language will be realized in the work of a technical university, which will improve the quality of education and increase the students’ interest in learning a foreign language for professional purposes. A preliminary survey was conducted among the teaching staff of the Foreign Languages Department on the effectiveness and relevance of applying such technologies in the educational process. According to the survey results, the majority of respondents supported the digital technologies’ usage and saw no obstacles to their implementation.

title: Trustworthy Autonomous RAN Orchestration: An Architectural Framework for Zero-Trust Intent-Based Control with Explainable AI
authors: Mukesh Dua, Tarun Kundu, Ankush Gupta
abstract: As sixth-generation (6G) networks move toward more autonomous orchestration, operators face a key challenge where they need to use AI-driven, intent-based systems across different vendors, but they do not have clear ways to verify how policies are created, review decision logic, or build trust without sharing sensitive network data. We introduce a framework combining zero-trust principles with intent-based RAN orchestration, explainable AI (XAI), and federated threat detection. The framework is built on four layers: (1) Intent Translation, where LLM agents decompose high-level intents into domain rules with full reasoning provenance; (2) Zero-Trust Validation, which checks policies against 3GPP standards [1], [2], [3], history, and network state before deployment; (3) Federated Trust Coordination, which enables edge-trained threat detection while keeping raw data private; and (4) Auditable Orchestration, that links every decision to its source, model version, and fallback options. We also lay out architectural patterns that help multi-vendor RICs work together, explain how to detect model drift before it becomes a problem, and offer safe ways for autonomous networks to explore and learn on their own. Instead of relying on opaque, black-box automation, this framework builds AI-driven RAN control on clear verification, transparent reasoning, and distributed trust, giving operators the confidence they need to deploy next-generation networks.

title: Beyond Autotuning: A Gamified Learning Path for Deep Understanding of Control Systems
authors: Sergey Mezin, Anatoly Kosoy, Vladislav Belyaev
publish: 2026 8th International Conference on Information Technologies in Engineering Education (Inforino)
abstract: A significant gap exists between the theoretical study of automatic control and the practical skills required to tune regulators in real-world industrial settings, such as power plants. For safety reasons, students are prohibited from conducting experiments on live systems, even during internships. While mathematical software packages are used for training, they often provide an incomplete experience, typically involving ready-made transfer functions and idealized simulation environments. This paper presents a novel gamified training simulator designed to bridge this gap. The system is built around a Programmable Logic Controller (PLC) that realistically simulates a technological process, coupled with an operator panel that allows connection of a student's laptop. The simulator imposes real-world constraints: experiments must stay within safe process limits, measurement data contains various noises (additive, multiplicative, outliers, drift), and the data acquisition rate emulates different controller grades (from 2 seconds to 0.1 seconds). Students must design experiments, properly filter and process data to identify a plant model, and then tune a regulator of arbitrary structure (P, PI, PID, positional, custom). The final evaluation is conducted not on the student's identified model, but on the "real" model inside the PLC. A scoring system based on setup time, number of alarms triggered, and key control quality indices fosters competition among students and against machine optimization algorithms. This approach, moving "beyond autotuning," creates a deep, practical, and engaging learning path for mastering control systems.

title: Editors' Introduction

title: Liquid Democracy: An Annotated Reading List

title: Guessing-Flipping Decoding Framework for Short Polar Codes in 6G
authors: Xuanyu Li, Yuxin Han, Kai Niu, Bolin Wu, Jiankui Yang
abstract: The guessing random additive noise decoding (GRAND) algorithm is known for its maximum likelihood performance and high throughput, yet there remains potential for optimization in specific coding schemes, as GRAND is only suitable for high-rate codes. For this, we introduce the guessing-flipping (GF) decoding framework, where error patterns (EPs) are continuously used to flip the most unreliable bits like GRAND, but an extra baseline decoder, like successive cancellation with list (SCL), is utilized on the flipped signal to determine if the correct codeword is obtained or another EP should be applied. The simulation results demonstrate that the GF-SCL decoder achieves improved performance and low complexity, practical in 6G traffic and control channels.

title: Martian Mineral Formation Analysis Based on Martian Mineral Corpus and Knowledge Graph_supp1-3702055.pdf
authors: Yanfei Zhong
abstract: Martian minerals are key indicators of the planet’s geological evolution and potential habitability. Their formation mechanisms record critical evidence of early aqueous activity, surface geochemistry, and geological activities. However, analyzing these mechanisms requires integrating heterogeneous data from spectroscopy, geomorphology, and stratigraphy. Existing studies still rely heavily on manual interpretation of heterogeneous data, resulting in inconsistent conclusions and a time-consuming reasoning process. To address this challenge, we build the first unified Martian Mineral Corpus and Martian Mineral Knowledge Graph (MMKG). The corpus integrates global Mars data products and scientific literature, while the MMKG unifies mineral-related concepts with their semantic and spatiotemporal relations, providing comprehensive data and knowledge support for reasoning. On this basis, RBG<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> is proposed as an intelligent question answering (QA) framework designed for Martian mineral analysis. RBG<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> performs knowledge graph path retrieval under dual constraints: geological vectors distilled from the unified corpus guide multi-hop exploration, and geological knowledge-consistency validates candidate chains. This yields graph paths that are both context-aligned and causally plausible. Leveraging these paths as the backbone, RBG<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> integrates geological context from the corpus and the knowledge graph to perform unified reasoning. Experimental results show that RBG<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> outperforms existing methods on Martian mineral question answering. For mineral formation mechanisms, RBG<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> covers over 90% of key points of experts’ analysis on average and produces reasoning that is both interpretable and traceable, achieving the first reliable and intelligent Martian mineral analysis. Code has been made available at: https://github.com/guoluqingfeng10086/MMQA.

title: A 27-year Longitudinal Study on the Structure of the Internet Topology: Scale-freeness and Preferential Attachment
authors: Wei Koong Chai, Assemgul Kozhabek, Benjamin Dodwell
abstract: In this paper, we present a 27-year longitudinal study of the Internet topology at Autonomous System (AS)-level, from 1998 to 2025. The Internet has been claimed to be scale-free exhibiting power-law degree distribution. This claim has been challenged from several fronts (e.g., rigor of the statistical method, assumptions made on finiteness of the network, completeness of available data). Using the latest statistical methodology, we revisit and verify whether this claim still holds. Using power-law as the reference distribution, we use maximum-likelihood fitting method with goodness-of-fit tests based on the Kolmogorov-Smirnov statistic and likelihood ratios to identify the best fitting distribution among several common statistical distributions. In fact, being scale-free directly implies the property would remain over the passage of time and hence, we investigate how this has evolved over the past nearly three decades.We found that power-law is not the best fit for the degree distribution of the Internet. For most years, truncated power-law offers better fit while log-normal is often better than power-law. Furthermore, the simple preferential attachment model has been attributed as the underlying generative mechanism that consequently results in the topology we have today. Based on the two methods we used, we found that while there is indication of preferential attachment as previously discovered, this is only true for smaller values of degree rather than the entire distribution and that the attachment behavior hovers between both sub-linear and super-linear attachment regimes at different time points. Finally, we also found that new links do not only tend to attach to high-degree nodes. There is also a large number of new links that join low-degree nodes, contradicting the claim on new nodes prefer to join high-degree nodes. Nevertheless, when weighted by the number of nodes with specific degree ranges, the net effect of “rich-gets-richer” still stands due to imbalance node population across different degrees.

title: An Analytical Theory of Avalanche Breakdown in p-n Junctions Derived From Semiconductor Band Structure_supp1-3698391.pdf
authors: Yaping Dan
abstract: This article presents a comprehensive analytical theory for the avalanche breakdown process in p-n junction diodes to address the critical reliance on empirical models in the design of avalanche photodiodes (APDs) and single-photon avalanche diodes (SPADs). The proposed model is derived from fundamental carrier continuity equations and impact excitation probability. Eventually, it yields closed-form expressions for the avalanche current density and the breakdown voltage. It demonstrates excellent agreement with experimental current–voltage characteristics across various semiconductors (Si, GaN, 4H-SiC, and InP) and a wide temperature range. TCAD simulations are also adopted to validate this model. The theory establishes a unified, physics-based criterion for defining the avalanche breakdown voltage, revealing its functional dependence on material parameters (bandgap, dielectric constant, and mean-free path) and doping concentrations. Significantly, it explicitly predicts the theoretical minimum and maximum limits for the breakdown voltage in a given material, which were well known but only implicitly reflected in previous empirical models. This work provides a rigorous theoretical framework for the steady-state dark avalanche current for the targeted design of diverse avalanche-based devices, ranging from low-breakdown-voltage SPADs for integrated quantum photonics to high-breakdown-voltage power devices, moving beyond trial-and-error approaches.

title: Dual-Mode Linear Cross-Polarization Converter-Based Highly Sensitive Refractive Index Sensor_supp1-3700132.docx
authors: SARTHAK SINGHAL
abstract: A multiarm Z-shaped resonator and slot-based wideband dual-mode terahertz (THz) linear cross-polarization converter (LCPC) for refractive index-based biosensing applications is proposed. It has a unitcell volume of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$0.134\lambda _{\mathrm {L}} \times 0.093\lambda _{\mathrm {L}} \times 0.061\lambda _{\mathrm {L}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\lambda _{\mathrm {L}} =148.5$</tex-math> </inline-formula> <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mu $</tex-math> </inline-formula>m) while exhibiting reflective polarization conversion ratio (PCR<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${}_{\mathrm {rx}}) \ge 80$</tex-math> </inline-formula>% across 2.02–6.59 THz and transmissive polarization conversion ratio (PCR<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${}_{\mathrm {tx}}) \ge 80$</tex-math> </inline-formula>% across the spectrum of 3.67–3.91 THz and 6.43–7.29 THz. Physical phenomenon behind the polarization conversions is studied through electric field distribution. It has stable PCR<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${}_{\mathrm {rx}} \ge 80$</tex-math> </inline-formula>% performance for incidence angles up to 30°. Its maximal sensitivity (S<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${}_{\max }$</tex-math> </inline-formula>) as a refractive index sensor is 618 GHz/RIU using PCR<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">rx</sub> and 792 GHz/RIU using PCR<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">tx</sub>. Its biosensor configuration has a S<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">max</sub> of 4.95 THz/RIU (PCR<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${}_{\mathrm {tx}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>) and 16.76 THz/RIU (PCR<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${}_{\mathrm {rx}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>). The proposed polarization converter’s wideband performance, dual-mode sensing capability, high sensitivity, and compact dimensions indicate that it is suitable for a variety of applications, such as wireless communication, biosensing, imaging, and security screening.

title: Accurate Approximation of Higher-Order Statistics of Channel Capacity in Log-Normal Fading
authors: Yassine Mouchtak, Faissal El Bouanani, Meiling Li
abstract: This paper investigates the higher-order statistics (HOS) of channel capacity (CC) in log-normal (LN) fading channels. Unlike prior works that rely on infinite series or highly sensitive approximations, we propose a simple closed-form approximation of the HOS of CC and its asymptotic expression in the high signal-to-noise ratio regime, based on which the amount of dispersion and the corresponding reliability of the signal throughput have been evaluated. The accuracy of the proposed expressions is verified through numerical simulations, showing excellent agreement with exact results across a wide range of channel parameters. These findings offer practical insights for the design of reliable communication systems over LN fading channels, while significantly reducing computational complexity.

title: LLM-Guided Adaptive Compensator: Bringing Adaptivity to Robotic Feedback Control With Large Language Model_supp2-3700674.mp4
authors: Yuxi Lu
abstract: Recent advances in code generation and reasoning have enabled the growing use of large language models (LLMs) in robotics. The design of traditional robotic feedback controllers often requires extensive expert knowledge and iterative computational effort, motivating the exploration of LLM-assisted controller development. However, existing efforts are largely restricted to overly simplified systems, provide limited comparison with human-designed controllers, and lack validation on real-world robotic platforms. To address these gaps, this work investigates the use of LLMs in robotic feedback control by focusing on adaptive control and proposing an LLM-guided adaptive compensator. Instead of generating a complete controller from scratch, the LLM is guided to design a compensator that modulates the response of an unknown system to match that of a predefined reference system, thereby achieving adaptivity. The proposed approach is evaluated using five adaptive controllers on a two-degree-of-freedom (DoF) soft robot and a one-DoF shoulder joint of a humanoid robot. Both simulation and real-world experiments demonstrate that the LLM-guided adaptive compensator achieves performance comparable to four conventional adaptive controllers. Lyapunov-based analysis further establishes the stability and generalization capability of the proposed compensator, while analysis of the reasoning process suggests a more structured design approach. This study introduces a novel framework for integrating LLMs into robotic feedback control, with promising practical applicability. Note to Practitioners—This paper proposes a framework that uses large language models (LLMs) to design an adaptive compensator for robotic feedback control. Existing LLM-based approaches have largely been limited to simple systems and to adjusting controller gains, which restricts their reliability and applicability in real automation settings. In contrast, the proposed framework enables an LLM to design a compensator that modulates the response of an existing controller to track a predefined reference system, thereby achieving adaptivity. We provide theoretical analysis, drawing on Lyapunov stability theory and analysis of the LLM reasoning process, to demonstrate the stability and generalization capability of the compensator. We also compare its performance with that of several human-designed adaptive controllers, showing comparable control performance along with improved usability and design efficiency. These results support validation on real-world robots, where both simulations and prototype experiments confirm improved performance. From a practical standpoint, the proposed approach is zero-shot, requires only the desired system response as input, and uses open prompts that generalize across different robot platforms. This eliminates the need for a complete system model. Although demonstrated on robotic feedback control, the framework is applicable to other feedback control domains. A current limitation is that the upper bound of system complexity that can be reliably handled remains to be established, motivating future benchmarking efforts.

title: ANN-Cache: Accelerating Approximate Nearest Neighbor Search Via Caching_supp1-3700562.pdf
authors: Lingli Li
abstract: Approximate nearest neighbor (ANN) search in the high-dimensional Euclidean space is a pivotal problem for various data science and AI applications. The performance of traditional in-memory ANN methods decreases dramatically when handling large-scale data due to their substantial expansion of main memory. Recently, disk-resident ANN methods have drawn considerable attention to enable large-scale ANN search. However, despite their significant improvements, they still suffer from high query latency due to heavy I/O cost. In this paper, we propose <sc xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">ANN-Cache</small>, an I/O-efficient cache-conscious ANN framework that leverages the skew in query workloads to reduce I/O cost. To the best of our knowledge, we are the first to formally study exploring caching to accelerate disk-based ANN search. <sc xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">ANN-Cache</small> consists of two components: (i) a discriminator that checks whether a query's ANN points are cached; and (ii) an LSH-based cache that stores historical queries and their results to support efficient ANN search. Experimental results indicate that <sc xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">ANN-Cache</small> achieves 3×-9× speedup over baselines while maintaining comparable or better accuracy, incurring relatively low extra memory overhead, and demonstrating strong adaptivity to dynamic workloads.

title: Resilient Edge Intelligence for Industrial IoT: A Non-Parametric MoE-Based Training–Inference Co-Design Framework for Multimodal Data
authors: Yu Lu, Xiaorong Zhu
abstract: In 6G-enabled Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT) systems, edge intelligence faces heterogeneous resource availability and highly uncertain operating conditions, which undermine semantic fault tolerance and low-latency inference. To this end, we propose a resilient edge intelligence framework with training–inference co-design, based on a system-level Non-Parametric Mixture of Experts (NP-MoE) in which each expert is instantiated as an execution-capable inference pipeline deployed on an edge node, and a latency-aware gating policy activates only a subset of experts per request for conditional computation. To avoid gradient-heavy parametric MoE training and gating updates, the framework uses lightweight modality-specific backbones together with a non-parametric semantic-evidence aggregation mechanism based on feature memory banks and statistical semantic-score alignment, where “non-parametric” refers to the absence of additional trainable gating or fusion parameters rather than parameter-free feature backbones, enabling retraining-free and robust multimodal inference under modality missingness. Across the lifecycle, split learning serves as a resource mapper to enable feasible model partitioning in resource-constrained edge environments, while the deployed experts form a distributed expert pool for elastic inference scheduling. Then the joint cooptimization of model partitioning and multi-domain resource allocation is formulated as a MINLP and solved via a hybrid graph-theoretic and convex optimization method to minimize lifecycle latency. Experiments on industrial datasets show that the proposed framework achieves a favorable accuracy–latency trade-off and remains robust under extreme modality missingness, outperforming representative multimodal fusion baselines.

title: QANet: A Neural Framework for Quality Assessment of Instance Segmentation in Microscopy Imaging
authors: Amit Aharoni, Assaf Arbelle, Michael Sidorov, Eliav Elul, Tammy Riklin Raviv
publish: IEEE Open Journal of Signal Processing
abstract: Reliable instance segmentation of cells in microscopy images is essential for quantitative imaging analysis, yet estimating segmentation quality on unseen data, though fundamental, remains largely overlooked. In practice, performance assessment typically requires manual ground-truth annotations, which are costly and impractical at scale. We address this evaluation gap by introducing QANet, a neural framework for post-hoc quality assessment of instance segmentations. Unlike segmentation models, QANet does not predict masks; instead, it receives an image and a segmentation produced by any method and estimates a quantitative quality score that approximates standard evaluation metrics, without requiring ground-truth annotations at inference time. QANet is model-agnostic and formulated as a regression task over image–mask pairs. It is built on the RibCage architecture, which performs multi-scale comparison between image content and segmentation structure, enabling sensitivity to both global shape consistency and fine boundary errors. Training is performed using synthetically perturbed segmentations with known quality scores, enabling supervision across controlled error modes while eliminating the need for large annotated failure datasets. We evaluate QANet on 2D and 3D datasets from the Cell Segmentation Benchmark and demonstrate accurate prediction of both overlap-based measures and boundary-sensitive metrics across multiple segmentation methods. The code is available at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/amita1996/QANet</uri>.

title: Transfer Learning-Based Explainable Deep Learning Model for Lung Cancer Detection Using CT Scan Images
authors: Sadek Al Prince, Gazi Mehraj Sakib, Aziz Misher Mishu
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Quantum Photonics, Artificial Intelligence &amp; Networking (QPAIN)
abstract: Lung cancer is among the most common causes of cancer death in the entire world. Therefore, timely and precise diagnosis of lung cancer is essential in facilitating the survival chances of patients. Computer-aided diagnosis (CAD) systems based on deep learning have demonstrated outstanding potential in recent years to enable automatic medical image analysis. The research paper would suggest an effective and explicable deep transfer learning system of multi-class lung cancer based on computed tomography (CT) images. Several state-of-the-art convolutional neural network (CNN) models have been implemented and tested to make a comparative analysis; these are DenseNet121, ResNet50, ResNet101, ResNet152, VGG19, EfficientNetB4, InceptionV3, and a custom CNN. The highest-performing one was the DenseNet121 model, which has an accuracy of 99.55 %, a precision of 0.9944, a recall of 0.9960, and an F1-score of 0.9952. This is due to the high level of performance that is caused by dense connectivity of DenseNets, which promotes feature reuse and reinforcement of gradient flow that facilitates better learning of complex patterns in lung tissue. Explainable artificial intelligence (XAI) methods, such as Grad-CAM++ and Sharp-CAM, were also incorporated to generate salient points in the CT images to ensure that the model emphasizes clinically significant points. Experimental findings indicate that the suggested DenseNet121-based system is robust, trustworthy, and very efficient with regard to automated classification of lung cancers; thus, it is a potentially viable decisionsupport methodology to aid radiologists in early diagnosis and detection of lung cancer.

title: A GaN Device with Self-Generated Negative Voltage for Enhanced Turn-Off Reliability
authors: Xinyang Liu, Rongxing Lai, Yun Dai, Xudong Li, Wen Xiao, Wenhong He, Zekun Zhou, Bo Zhang, Gaofei Tang, Wen Shi, Zhiwen Dong, Qimeng Jiang
publish: 2026 IEEE 38th International Symposium on Power Semiconductor Devices and ICs (ISPSD)
abstract: This paper presents a novel self-generated negative voltage turn-off Gallium Nitride (SelfNeg-GaN) device. It features a power-free negative voltage charge pump monolithically integrated into a power GaN transistor, requiring only a small co-packaged capacitor to form a standard 3-terminal discrete GaN device. The SelfNeg-GaN generates a negative voltage pulse at its internal gate during turn-off, effectively suppressing false turn-on and ringing while avoiding prolonged negative gate bias. Fabricated using a 650 V 0.5 µm E-GaN process, the device maintains the static characteristics of conventional GaN devices and achieves a peak negative gate voltage of -4.4 V with a duration of 265 ns during turn-off. These advantages are further validated through double-pulse testing, confirming the device’s superior turn-off reliability.

title: An Accuracy and Computational Performance Study of Basis, Operator, and Curvilinear Implementations of DGTD for Maxwell’s equations
authors: Alejandro Muñoz Manterola, Luis Manuel Díaz Angulo, Alberto Gascón Bravo, Carlos Julio Ramos Salas, Farah Amador, Salvador González García
publish: IEEE Transactions on Antennas and Propagation
abstract: We present an accuracy and computational-performance study of Discontinuous Galerkin Time-Domain (DGTD) variants for Maxwell’s equations. The comparison covers: (i) open Legendre–Gauss (LG) versus closed Legendre–Gauss–Lobatto (LGL) nodal bases, (ii) a fully assembled global sparse evolution operator versus a matrix-light connectivity-based (Hesthaven-inspired) operator, and (iii) linear versus second-order curvilinear elements. Validation is performed on three representative benchmarks: (A) a 2D PEC cavity excited with mode TM55, (B) a 2D circular cavity initialized with a Bessel J6 mode, and (C) the 3D monostatic Radar Cross-Section (RCS) of a PEC sphere to evaluate curved-element benefits against an analytical Mie-series reference. Beyond error convergence, the benchmark sections report representative sparsity through the number of non-zero entries (NNZ), together with the runtime and memory indicators relevant to each comparison. The results show that open and closed bases deliver comparable field accuracy where they are directly compared, while LGL yields systematically sparser operators and, in the reported TM55 and 3D RCS runs, lower memory/runtime cost in the studied high-order regimes. For TM55, open-versus-closed temporal-evolution memory differences reach about 64% at the highest spatially refined case (H3) with a basis order of p = 10, and global-versus-local memory differences reach about 88% in favor of the local operator at the same configuration. Curvilinear elements are essential to remove geometry-induced error floors in curved-domain benchmarks; their computational overhead is benchmark- and configuration-dependent rather than universal. These results provide practical guidance for selecting bases, operators, and geometric representations in large-scale, highfidelity time-domain electromagnetics.

title: Attention-Driven Alaryngeal Speech Enhancement via Discrete Representation Learning and Timbre-Preserving Augmentation
authors: Hnin Yadana Lwin, Wuttipong Kumwilaisak, Chatchawarn Hansakunbuntheung, Nattanun Thatphithakkul
publish: IEEE Open Journal of the Computer Society
abstract: Alaryngeal speech enhancement seeks to restore intelligibility and naturalness for individuals who have undergone laryngectomy. Existing methods based on direct spectral mapping or frame-level voice conversion fail to exploit speech's linguistic structure and are constrained by scarce pathological data. We propose a multi-stage framework integrating discrete representation learning and attention-driven feature alignment. WavLM embeddings are quantized via <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$k$</tex-math></inline-formula>-means and refined by a Speech Language Model that maps alaryngeal tokens toward normal-speech tokens, followed by Conformer-based reconstruction. An Attention-Driven Feature Matcher (ADFM) then refines features through three coupled modules: a residual Feature Enhancer with learnable scaling, a Multi-Head Attention Matcher performing soft alignment over <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$k$</tex-math></inline-formula>-nearest normal-speech neighbors, and a Quality Predictor that estimates per-frame confidence as an inference-time reliability indicator. A HiFi-GAN vocoder synthesizes the waveform. To overcome data scarcity, we introduce a Multi-Mask GAN augmentation strategy extending MaskCycleGAN-VC with a timbre similarity loss for speaker-identity preservation. Evaluations on Thai and English corpora show best speaker similarity in both languages (0.858 Thai, 0.850 English) with competitive or state-of-the-art FAD (5.252 Thai, 5.385 English) and MCD (5.644 Thai, 5.322 English), yielding up to 27.3% FAD reduction over kNN-VC. Subjective tests confirm significant listener preference (77% English, 67% Thai).

title: Safety-Certified Cooperative Path Following of Networked Autonomous Surface Vehicles Based on Nash Equilibrium Seeking With Experiments
authors: Yanping Xu, Lu Liu, Bing Han, Anqing Wang, Zhouhua Peng
abstract: This article addresses the safety-certified cooperative path following of networked underactuated autonomous surface vehicles (ASVs) with individual interests navigating complex marine environments. Each ASV operates under static/dynamic obstacle constraints, neighboring ASV constraints, and input constraints. A three-layer safety-certified path-following architecture is proposed based on control barrier functions and Nash equilibrium seeking. At the communication layer, a path variable update law based on distributed Nash equilibrium seeking is designed to achieve path variable cooperation. At the nominal layer, a line-of-sight-based principle is employed to design guidance laws for each ASV to achieve path following. At the optimization layer, three heading-constrained control barrier functions are developed for mapping safety constraints to constraints on yaw rates of ASVs, and a quadratic program is constructed to optimize the guidance yaw rate signal. The distinct features of the proposed method are two aspects: each ASV is allowed to consider its individual interest when performing a group task; static/dynamic obstacles and neighboring ASVs can be avoided by changing the yaw rate only. Stability analysis demonstrates that the feedback control system exhibits input-to-state stability. The efficacy of the novel method is validated through both hardware-in-the-loop simulation results and field experiment results.

title: Multi-Dimensional Perception of Two-Channel Skin-Stretch: Effects of Cue Pattern and Spatial Configuration
authors: Ching Hei Cheng, Jonathan Eden, Denny Oetomo, Ying Tan
abstract: Assistive robots lack natural proprioceptive feedback, often forcing users to rely on vision. With recent advances, haptic interfaces can now accommodate the multidimensional nature of proprioceptive information. Skin-stretch is one promising haptic modality to provide proprioceptive information that resembles natural body mechanisms and conveys both magnitude and direction information, enabling compact multi-channel designs. While prior studies have examined perceptibility and channel interference, the ability of participants to integrate information across multiple channels remains not well understood. This study evaluates how effectively users can combine two skin-stretch channels to interpret two-dimensional cues. 21 able-bodied participants judged, in collocated and non-collocated placement, the linear projection of stimuli under four patterns of skin-stretch variation. Participants could generally perceive and integrate both channels into a coherent understanding of two-dimensional cues, though some non-uniformity in interpretation was observed. Channel proximity showed no clear effect on performance or workload. These findings provide the first systematic investigation of multi-channel integration in skin-stretch feedback and inform the design of scalable proprioceptive haptic feedback interfaces for assistive robots.

title: Open-Circuit Fault Diagnosis of Voltage Source Inverters Based on Microfeature Extraction and Third-Order Tracking Differentiator
authors: Hongyi Guo, Chonghui Song, Zhengkuo Jiao, Zhenjie Xu, Qiuyang Xu, Yucong Zhang, Shuo Li, Naizhe Diao
abstract: This article proposes an open-circuit (OC) fault diagnosis method for voltage source inverters (VSIs) based on microfeature extraction and a third-order tracking differentiator. The proposed method is designed for complex operating conditions such as variable frequency and variable load. It enables effective diagnosis of both types I and II faults. First, a PWM-aligned masked averaging filter (PMAF) is developed. By constructing two averaging windows within each carrier period and masking the intermediate interval, the proposed filter effectively suppresses noise while preserving fault features. Second, a third-order linear tracking differentiator (LTD3) is employed to extract the second-order derivative of currents. The fault features are mapped into a unified second-order derivative domain. As a result, a unified fault criterion is established, eliminating the need for type-specific detection logic. On this basis, a unified fault diagnosis function is constructed to achieve fault detection and fault localization. Compared with existing methods, the proposed method shows improved diagnostic response speed, and maintains robustness under complex operating conditions, with an algorithmic complexity of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\bm{O}\mathbf{(1)}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>. Both simulation and experimental results validate the effectiveness of the proposed method.

title: A Unified Full Speed Domain Rotor Position and Speed Estimation Method Based on Adaptive Multifrequency State Observer for IPMSM Sensorless Drives
authors: Yanping Zhang, Zhonggang Yin, Fengtao Gao, Jing Liu
abstract: A unified full speed domain rotor position and speed estimation method based on an adaptive multifrequency state observer (AMFSO) for interior permanent magnet synchronous motors (IPMSMs) sensorless drives is proposed in this article. First, the AMFSO is used to estimate the fundamental frequency back electromotive force (BEMF) and high frequency (HF) BEMF. The fundamental frequency BEMF estimation loop of AMFSO has bandpass characteristics without phase shift at the motor operating frequency, and has notch filter characteristics at the frequency of injecting HF voltage. The HF BEMF estimation loop of AMFSO has bandpass characteristics without amplitude attenuation or phase shift at the frequency of injecting HF voltage, and has notch filter characteristics at the motor operating frequency. Therefore, AMFSO can accurately estimate the fundamental frequency BEMF and HF BEMF. Then, the full speed domain unified virtual BEMF model is established, and the rotor position and speed in the full speed domain are estimated by the unified virtual BEMF model. There is no need to switch the two estimation methods, and the established unified virtual BEMF model has no phase jump when HF voltage is injected or not. Finally, the effectiveness of the proposed method was validated on an IPMSM driver platform.

title: Temporal-State-Switching of Self-Sweeping Fiber Lasers
authors: Kaile Wang, Zifan Li, Ping Wang, Zengrun Wen, Tian Cao
abstract: The self-sweeping effect originates from dynamic gratings established when a doped fiber within a laser resonator is subjected to a standing-wave field. In the temporal domain, this effect can be classified into two distinct regimes: the microsecond-pulse (MP) state and the continuous-wave (CW) state, with the principal criterion of differentiation being whether the doped fiber responsible for dynamic grating formation is pump-excited. Elucidating the underlying generation mechanisms of these two self-sweeping laser regimes and realizing controllable switching between their temporal states constitute a critical research focus in this field. In this work, a CW self-sweeping output was achieved by inducing dynamic gratings in an unpumped doped fiber (UDF) incorporated within a unidirectional ring cavity, and controllable transition from the CW regime to the MP self-sweeping regime was subsequently realized by introducing pump excitation to the UDF. Systematic experiments were conducted under four conditions by independently regulating the pump powers of the gain-doped fiber (GDF) and UDF (pumping only GDF/only UDF, fixing GDF pump power to adjust UDF power, fixing UDF pump power to adjust GDF power). Furthermore, a theoretical model of the CW self-sweeping laser was formulated based on rate equations. By systematically varying the gain of the UDF, the transition mechanism from CW to MP self-sweeping operation was elucidated. Both theoretical and experimental results demonstrate that progressively increasing the UDF pump power induces a regime transition of the laser from the CW state to the MP state, accompanied by a pronounced enhancement of the pulse repetition rate throughout the transition. This investigation overcomes the inherent limitation of conventional lasers restricted to a single temporal operating mode, achieves controllable switching between CW and MP outputs, and thereby provides a novel paradigm for the development of high-performance self-sweeping laser sources.

title: Receiver-Tilt-Robust RSS-Based VLP System Using a Normal Orthogonal Three-PD (NO3PD) Positioning Scheme
authors: Zhengliang Wang, Hang Xu, Aiying Yang, Yaojun Qiao
abstract: In received signal strength (RSS)-based visible light positioning (VLP) systems, receiver tilt directly affects the measured RSS and introduces bias into positioning estimates. To mitigate this issue, a normal orthogonal three-PD (NO3PD) structure is proposed to receive the positioning signal, where three PDs are arranged with mutually perpendicular normals. Theoretically, using the root- mean-square (RMS) of the RSS values from the NO3PD receiver for positioning eliminates the influence of tilt. To further reduce the complexity of the RSS extraction method, a cross-correlation-computed RSS (cccRSS) extraction method is proposed. To validate the robustness of the proposed NO3PD-based scheme against receiver tilt, we compare it with a single-PD-based scheme through simulations and experiments. Simulation results show that in a 100 cm × 100 cm × 160 cm space with the receiver tilt angle ranging from <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$0^{\circ }$</tex-math></inline-formula> to <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$15^{\circ }$</tex-math></inline-formula>, the NO3PD-based scheme achieves a maximum average positioning error of 2.91 cm with a range of 0.39 cm, while the single-PD-based scheme achieves 4.17 cm and 1.78 cm, respectively. Experimental results in a similarly sized space, with the receiver tilt angle ranging from <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$0^{\circ }$</tex-math></inline-formula> to <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$13.1^{\circ }$</tex-math></inline-formula>, show that the NO3PD-based scheme attains a maximum average positioning error of 4.67 cm and a range of 0.98 cm, compared to 6.78 cm and 3.37 cm for the single-PD scheme. To assess the time performance of cccRSS extraction method, we implement the method on a microcontroller unit (MCU) and compare it with a fast Fourier transform (FFT)-based method, showing that it consumes only 6.4 % of the computation time of the latter. Furthermore, at a receiver speed of 35 cm/s without tilt, real-time positioning using the NO3PD-based scheme achieves an average positioning error of 3.93 cm, which is consistent with the previous error. Overall, the proposed NO3PD-based scheme exhibits smaller error ranges under tilt variation and achieves centimeter-level real-time positioning, demonstrating its superior tilt robustness and real-time capability.

title: Boots, Bulls, and Breakthroughs: What the Houston MedTech Rodeo Taught Me About the Future of Our Industry
authors: Chad Andresen

title: Cross-Layer Throughput Analysis of Hybrid-Access Wi-Fi in Time-Varying Channels
authors: S. Arthi, Neelesh B. Mehta
abstract: Conventional Wi-Fi standards employ contention-based access, in which a user adapts the modulation and coding scheme (MCS) based on slow medium access control (MAC)-layer feedback. On the other hand, the current-generation IEEE 802.11ax standard employs hybrid access, in which the access point (AP) uses trigger frames to schedule users over orthogonal resource units (RUs) and controls their MCSs in a more agile manner to achieve a higher spectral efficiency. We develop a novel, cross-layer analysis that accurately characterizes the saturation throughput of hybrid access in time-varying channels. Unlike the literature, our approach accounts for channel state information (CSI) acquisition delays and their dependence on RU allocation and the number of scheduled users, which vary with time. Our results reveal the extent to which the CSI delays degrade the throughput due to the timescale of operation of the 802.11ax MAC. We also propose a throughput-optimal MCS selection rule that effectively mitigates the impact of CSI delays.

title: A Generalized Precise Modeling Framework for High-Step-Up Resonant Converters
authors: Qianxiang Shen, Enhui Chu, Pengcheng Wang, Chenghao Sun, Qiuye Sun
abstract: To achieve miniaturization and high step-up in resonant converters, high resonant frequencies and high transformer turns ratios are required, resulting in small primary magnetizing inductance and large secondary winding capacitance. This leads to the transition from mainstream three-element resonant topologies (e.g., LLC, LCC) to more complex four element resonant modes, for which existing analytical methods become inadequate. This paper proposes a generalized precise modeling framework for such high-resonant-frequency high step-up (HRF-HSU) converters. The framework abstracts the resonant behavior of four elements into sequences of two fixed operational stages, standardizing the modeling process across the entire switching frequency range. Models derived from this framework accurately describe resonant dynamics in any frequency band and precisely plot full-range gain-frequency characteristics. The accuracy of the proposed modeling framework is assessed against experimental data and compared with that of mainstream modeling methods, demonstrating its advantage in reducing error. Finally, using the calculated gain frequency curves and resonant tank state trajectories, a case study on switching frequency range selection and resonant parameter optimization is presented.

title: Volumetric Near-Field Electromagnetic Sensing of Creatinine in Urine Using a 3D-Printed Bifilar Helical Platform
authors: Rayan Al-Sayed Ali, Joseph Costantine, Youssef Tawk, Rouwaida Kanj, Joanna Ghaddar, Fatima Asadallah, Nassim Fares, Dania Chelala, Assaad A. Eid
abstract: This paper presents a microwave sensing platform for quantitative evaluation of creatinine concentration in urine using a 3D-printed bifilar helical resonant structure. The proposed sensing system integrates two conductive helices with a slotted cylindrical shell incorporating volumetric split-ring resonators, enabling enhanced confinement of near-field electromagnetic energy within the sensing region. This design strengthens the interaction between the electromagnetic fields and the urine sample, resulting in increased sensitivity to variations in creatinine concentration. The sensing structure is fabricated using precision additive manufacturing to ensure high reproducibility and stable electromagnetic performance. Operating in the 4–6.5 GHz frequency range, the sensor response is characterized through measured scattering parameters, which are processed using artificial intelligence techniques for both regression and classification tasks. Gaussian Process regression is employed to estimate creatinine concentration, achieving a mean absolute relative difference (MARD) of 10% for artificial urine samples, between 6.94% and 10% for ex-vivo rat studies, and 7.2% for clinical human urine samples. In addition, a support vector machine classifier is used to detect creatinine in the presence of common interferers, achieving an accuracy ranging from 92.5% to 97.5%, a sensitivity between 90% and 100%, and a specificity between 91.6% and 100%. The proposed platform demonstrates a robust, reagent-free, and noninvasive approach for real-time creatinine monitoring, offering promising potential for future point-of-care diagnostic applications and early assessment of kidney function.

title: Localizing the Epileptogenic Zone Using SEEG-Based Excitation-Inhibition Dynamics and Spectral Features in Drug-Resistant Epilepsy: A Multicenter Retrospective Study_supp1-3702844.pdf
authors: Yalin Wang

title: A Compact High-Performance MR Safe Pneumatic Stepper Motor
authors: Ziting Liang, Ryman Hashem, Chuang Lu, Mohamed E.M.K. Abdelaziz, Steve Bandula, Vincent Groenhuis, Stefano Stramigioli, Danail Stoyanov, Agostino Stilli
abstract: Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI)-guided interventions have advanced rapidly due to their superior soft-tissue contrast and real-time imaging capability. To enable actuation for robots and other medical devices used in tumour biopsy and ablation within magnetically sensitive environments, several MRI-compatible pneumatic motors have been developed using 3D printing. Although 3D printing enables the fabrication of pneumatic structures, maintaining airtightness and smooth piston motion within 3D-printed cylinders remains challenging due to tolerance variations and surface roughness. These issues lead to pressure losses, increased air consumption, and reduced torque output during motor operation. To address these challenges, this paper presents a compact, high-performance, easily manufacturable MR Safe pneumatic stepper motor that circumvents 3D-printing tolerance limitations by employing prefabricated cylinders. The motor combines a replaceable design with great airtightness and high torque performance. The proposed design achieves negligible air leakage of 0.047 mL/min measured via a designed water displacement method and a maximum output torque of 1.04 Nm at 3.7 revolutions per minute (rpm) under 6 bar, without an additional gearbox. The motor has a compact design, 48 × 48 × 36 mm, which enables the motor to be integrated into the medical devices within the narrow MRI scanning bore.

title: Generative Feature Imputing—A Technique for Error-Resilient Semantic Communication
authors: Jianhao Huang, Qunsong Zeng, Hongyang Du, Kaibin Huang
abstract: Semantic communication (SemCom) has emerged as a promising paradigm for achieving unprecedented communication efficiency in sixth-generation (6G) networks by leveraging artificial intelligence (AI) to extract and transmit the underlying meanings of source data. However, deploying SemCom over digital systems presents new challenges, particularly in ensuring robustness against transmission errors that may distort semantically critical content. To address this issue, this paper proposes a novel framework, termed generative feature imputing, which comprises three key techniques. First, we introduce a spatial-error-concentration packetization strategy that spatially concentrates feature distortions by encoding feature elements based on their channel mappings—a property crucial for both the effectiveness and reduced complexity of the subsequent techniques. Second, building on this strategy, we propose a generative feature imputing method that utilizes a diffusion model to efficiently reconstruct missing features caused by packet losses. Finally, we develop a semantic-aware power allocation scheme that enables unequal error protection by allocating transmission power according to the semantic importance of each packet. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed framework outperforms conventional approaches, such as Deep Joint Source-Channel Coding (DJSCC) and JPEG2000, under block fading conditions, achieving higher semantic accuracy and lower Learned Perceptual Image Patch Similarity (LPIPS) scores.

title: Deep Uncertainty-Aware Tracking for Maneuvering Targets
authors: Shuyang Zhang, Chang Gao, Bo Chen, Qingfu Zhang, Shefeng Yan, Hongwei Liu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
abstract: Traditional target tracking approaches typically presuppose a target motion model. Once the target maneuvers, the model may get mismatched and is incapable of depicting the complex motion characteristics of the target. Most data-driven methods are designed based on a regression-only framework, which increases the difficulty of network training. Moreover, these methods are typically designed under the assumption of a fixed measurement noise distribution. However, the measurement noise distribution of actual radar is influenced by fluctuations in the target's radar cross section. Such fluctuations may lead to a degradation in tracking performance. In this paper, we propose a representation method for radar measurements, referred to as the measurement uncertainty projection operator (MUPO). It projects the measurement onto a target state space to capture the measurement noise distribution. Additionally, we propose a framework that reformulates maneuvering target tracking as a hybrid task of classification and regression. To realize the functionality of this framework, we establish a MUPO-based target tracking network. The rationality of the proposed network structure is supported by the validation experiment. Numerical results demonstrate that the proposed method achieves superior estimation precision compared with existing data-driven methods during target maneuvers. Furthermore, the proposed algorithm retains its superior tracking performance when extended to 3D maneuvering target tracking and multi-target tracking scenarios.

authors: Xiang Zhao, Ruge Zhang, Haipeng Jia, Kun Li, Jianliang Xu, Ting Cao, Yunxin Liu, Yunquan Zhang

title: Hybrid Kinematic and Muscular Null Space for Simultaneous Control of Natural and Extra Degrees of Freedom
authors: Julien Rossato, Bernardo Brogi, Tommaso Lisini Baldi, Elodie Hinnekens, Domenico Prattichizzo, Andrea D’Avella
publish: IEEE Transactions on Neural Systems and Rehabilitation Engineering
abstract: Humans possess more degrees of freedom than are required to perform upper-limb tasks, giving rise to a body null space that can be exploited to extend natural control capabilities. Both kinematic and muscular null space control modalities have been shown to be feasible in prior studies; however, their relative performance has not yet been compared within the same participants and task. Moreover, their potential combined use for flexible control of supernumerary degrees of freedom (SuDoFs) remains unexplored. In this study, we compare kinematic and muscular null space control and investigate a hybrid modality that either combines both to control one SuDoF or uses them separately to control two SuDoFs simultaneously. We propose a novel implementation of kinematic and muscular null space extraction that enables their real-time integration within a unified control framework. Sixteen participants performed a reaching and orientation task in a virtual reality environment, controlling the rotation of a virtual elliptical cursor to match targets with varying orientations. Task difficulty was modulated through single-axis and dual-axis target rotations, as well as with a baseline condition requiring no rotation control. Participants completed the task using muscular, kinematic, and hybrid null space control modalities. Results showed that muscular control outperformed purely kinematic control for rotated targets. The hybrid modality significantly improved performance relative to kinematic control, mitigating performance degradation, and supported both combined single-SuDoF control and simultaneous two-SuDoF control. These findings demonstrate that hybrid null space control is a robust and flexible strategy for human augmentation.

title: A General Analysis of Passive Component Sizing for Current-Sourced Buck Converters
authors: Qijia Li, Jonah Rawley, Samantha Coday
publish: IEEE Open Journal of Power Electronics
abstract: Power converter topologies with inductors at the input offer the potential to reduce conducted electromagnetic interference (EMI). To harness this benefit in converters conventionally implemented with inductors at the output, an input passive network consisting of two inductors and a bypass capacitor can be utilized instead of the output inductor. This work focuses on the current-sourced buck converter (CSBC), the most fundamental two input inductor topology, and presents a comprehensive analytical model for the bypass capacitor voltage and input inductor currents. In addition, an analytical expression for the output voltage is derived, demonstrating its dependence on not only the duty ratio but also the switching and resonant frequencies of the CSBC. The analysis is expanded from an uncoupled CSBC to include magnetic coupling between the input inductors, which enables the CSBC to be implemented with only one magnetic core. The effects of coupling are thoroughly analyzed, including the potential to reduce passive component volume. The proposed model is validated through simulation and experimental results for a design example. Design insights are provided to guide passive component value selection, evaluate switch stress based on capacitor voltage and inductor current ripples, and identify operating conditions where magnetic coupling may be beneficial in reducing passive component volume.

title: Tuberculosis Detection from Chest X-Rays: A Transformer-Based Deep Learning Approach for Faster and More Accurate Diagnosis
authors: Tanjid Hasan Turky, Fernaz Narin Nur
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Quantum Photonics, Artificial Intelligence &amp; Networking (QPAIN)
abstract: Tuberculosis (TB) is a serious problem worldwide, especially in low and middle-income countries where access to expert radiologists is low. Chest X-ray (CXR) screening is highly popular but manual interpretation is a time-consuming task and is subject to inter-observer variability. This study investigates transformer-based architectures for automated tuberculosis (TB) detection from chest X-ray images. A Swin Transformers model was evaluated with stratified 5-fold cross-validation on a publicly available dataset with 4200 images (700 TB and 3500 normal). To overcome class imbalance, cost-sensitive learning was applied with the use of class-weighted loss functions. On an internal test set held-out of 840 images (700 normal, 140 TB), the Swin Transformer obtained 99.29% accuracy, 0.96 TB sensitivity, 1.00 specificity and a TB F1-score of 0.98. Five fold cross validated performance was stable with narrow confidence interval. Out-of-domain testing on a separate data set of 3,008 images gave 85.17% accuracy, 0.8821 sensitivity, 0.7043 specificity which shows the effects of domain shift. Grad-CAM was carried out to give visual explanations and validate clinically relevant attention. Results suggest the proposed framework is an excellent candidate as a decision support tool for mass screening for TB, provided it is validated in additional multi-center studies and adaptation of the framework to additional domains is conducted.

title: Multi-Scale BiFPN Networks for Tiny Crop Pest Detection with Temporal Infestation Forecasting
authors: Papri Saha, Firoz Ahmed
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Quantum Photonics, Artificial Intelligence &amp; Networking (QPAIN)
abstract: Pest management in agriculture requires a system that can detect and predict outbreaks within agricultural settings. This study introduces a composite model consisting of a BiFPN-based EfficientNet-B4 detector and temporal forecasting models to detect tiny cotton pests and predict their population. Multi-scale feature fusion on <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$896 \times 896$</tex> images is used in the detection module to achieve <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$92.7 \% \text{mAP} {@} 0.5$</tex> on the PestNU dataset of aphids and whiteflies occupying less than 0.5 % of the image area. In our case of population forecasting, various models are tested involving population counts derived from detection and environmental factors (temperature, humidity, and rainfall). We demonstrate that the ability to forecast whiteflies gives the best performance in the Random Forest (RMSE <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$=2.22$</tex>, <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$R^{2}=\mathbf{8 2. 1 \%}$</tex>) and aphids in transformer architecture (RMSE <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$=\mathbf{0. 0 7}$</tex>, <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$R^{2}=23.9 \%$</tex>, accuracy <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$=75.9 \%$</tex>), which can be attributed to the different population dynamics of the species. The integrated system facilitates the connection between reactive detection and proactive prediction and allows for accurate pest management in agricultural environments.

title: EOCNet: Elongated Oriented Convolution for High-Aspect-Ratio Object Detection in SAR Imagery
authors: Peixuan Li, Ziqi Ye, Haipeng Wang
abstract: Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) object detection is hindered by intense speckle noise and targets with extreme aspect ratios. Traditional square kernels struggle to represent elongated structures and often capture redundant background clutter. We propose the Elongated Oriented Convolution Network (EOCNet), a dual-domain framework designed for robust SAR detection. In the frequency domain, Multi-level Wavelet Convolution (WTConv) achieves exponential receptive field expansion while suppressing high-frequency noise. In the spatial domain, Elongated Oriented Convolution (EOC) extracts directional features along primary axes to enhance narrow-structure representation. Furthermore, an optimized localization head embedded with EOC modules ensures precise boundary alignment. Experiments on MSAR-1.0, FAIR-CSAR, and SAR-Airport-1.0 datasets demonstrate that EOCNet outperforms state-of-the-art methods such as Oriented R-CNN and ReDet, achieving mAPs of 88.8%, 52.0%, and 99.6%, respectively.

title: An Intelligent AI Framework for Early Parkinson's Disease Diagnosis Using PCA-Enhanced Vocal Feature Engineering
authors: Mohammad Tahbib Ullah, Shanjida Hossain Shefa, Sk Tahmed Salim Rafid, Amit Saha, Md Aslamuzzaman Bappy, Md. Alif Sheakh
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Quantum Photonics, Artificial Intelligence &amp; Networking (QPAIN)
abstract: Parkinson's disease is a neurological condition that primarily impairs movement and motor function and significantly drops a patient's quality of life. Detecting PD early is necessary for effective disease management and timely medical intervention. In this research, we propose a machine learning system using vocal features as a non-invasive and accessible biomarker. Our study begins with exploratory data analysis, followed by feature selection methods to find the most impactful attributes. Then, we utilized several machine learning algorithms, including Decision Tree, Naïve Bayes, Support Vector Classifier, Random Forest, Logistic Regression,XGBoost, K-Nearest Neighbor, and AdaBoost, and calculated predictive performance using relevant metrics. To enhance our model performance, a hyperparameter tuning strategy is implemented to optimize the learning period. The XGBoost classifier, optimized through the principal component analysis feature selection technique and subsequent hyperparameter tuning, demonstrated robust performance. This work attained the highest accuracy of 97.44 % and shows that this fine-tuned model is both reliable and well-suited for the detection.

title: Predicting Brain Stroke Risk from Clinical Data using Optimized Machine Learning Models with Random Stochastic Hyperparameter Optimization
authors: Md. Mahinur Rashid, Md. Badhon Sikder, Shamim Kabir Kazim, Muhammad Minoar Hossain, Md. Sadiq Iqbal, Mohammad Mamun
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Quantum Photonics, Artificial Intelligence &amp; Networking (QPAIN)
