title: An Ethical AI Model for Bias Detection and Fair Decision-Making in Machine Learning Systems
authors: Anshu Vashisth, Gagandeep Kaur, Nahita Pathania, Richa Jain
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Intelligent Communication Technologies and Virtual Mobile Networks (ICICV)
abstract: The high rate of artificial intelligence and machine learning systems integration has changed the way decisions are made in different fields, including healthcare, finance, recruiting, and criminals. Nevertheless, the growing use of automated decision systems has brought considerable concerns of algorithmic bias, fairness, and ethical responsibility. The biased or imbalanced datasets used to train machine learning models might offer biased results that disadvantage some people or groups of people due to sensitive characteristics like gender, race, or socio-economic status. This paper suggests an Ethical AI Model to solve such problems, which is Bias Detection and Fair Decision-Making in Machine Learning Systems that incorporates the processes of fairness assessment and machine learning decision systems. The proposed model constantly examines training data, model predictions, and decision outcomes to determine possible bias and make equitable decision-making. The framework will have four significant components, i.e., Data Bias Detection Module, Fairness Evaluation Engine, Ethical Decision Model, and Continuous Monitoring Layer. Bias detection methods are used to detect dataset imbalance and feature correlations, and fairness measures are used to assess model predictions in demographic groups. The system is dynamic in that the model decisions are adjusted dynamically with fairness constraints to minimize discrimination in automated predictions. It was experimentally proven that the proposed model scores higher on fairness (94.3), the bias difference is 38.0 less, and the prediction accuracy of that model is greater than 92.0, which is higher than that of the traditional machine learning decision systems. These findings indicate that the suggested ethical AI framework is a reliable and scalable solution to ensuring fairness, transparency, and accountability in the current AI-based decision systems.

title: Envelope Elimination and Restoration Power Amplifier High-Frequency Path Computer Model Development
authors: Oleg V. Varlamov
publish: 2026 Wave Electronics and its Application in Information and Telecommunication Systems (WECONF)
abstract: A computer model has been developed to study specific distortions occurring in the high-frequency (HF) path of promising high-efficiency power amplifiers (PA) with Envelope Elimination and Restoration (EER). The studies carried out with its help showed that one of the main causes of specific distortions in the high-frequency EER PA path are free oscillations caused by shock excitation of the oscillatory circuit formed by the leakage inductance of a short-circuit transformer and final stage (FS) transistors nonlinear output capacitances. For accelerated attenuation of these oscillations, it is proposed to install damping RC chains in the FS drain circuits. The developed model also allows us to investigate the main cause of distortions in the EER PA HF path - the throughput power that comes from the final stage input to its output through the nonlinear throughput transistor capacitance. Developing solutions to reduce the impact of this effect will be areas of further research.

title: Edge-AI based Intelligent Road Monitoring System using YOLOv8 for Real-Time Traffic Analysis and Road Damage Detection
authors: M. Durgapoorani, S. Abinaya, J. Joel Jeffrey, P. Leninpugalhanthi
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Intelligent Communication Technologies and Virtual Mobile Networks (ICICV)
abstract: Road condition has a direct effect on the safety of transportation, travel efficiency, and prevention of accidents. Conventional monitoring approaches like human inspections or cloud-based image processing are either time-consuming, resource-hungry, or plagued by latency and high bandwidth consumption. This paper puts forward an edge-AI-based intelligent road monitoring system where computation is performed right on a Raspberry Pi board that is embedded with a camera and GPS module. The system uses the newest version of the "You Only Look Once" family of object detection algorithms, YOLOv8, to execute two key functionalities in real-time: vehicle detection for traffic index calculation and surface damage detection for pothole and crack detection. Preprocessing activities like grayscale conversion, noise reduction, and contrast enhancement are performed before inference for the detection to be strong under various light and environmental conditions. Severity of damage is assessed by merging YOLOv8 confidence scores with bounding-box area measurements and is classified as minor, moderate, or severe. By merging the severity scores with vehicle traffic density, the system automatically calculates priority values for road segments so that high-traffic roads with severe damages are identified for immediate repair. Critical incidents are transferred directly to the cloud along with GPS location and photograph evidence, but less critical information is recorded on-board and synchronized from time to time to conserve bandwidth.Authorities are given heatmaps, severity metrics, and prioritized alerts through a cloud dashboard to inform proactive road maintenance actions. Experimental comparison indicates that YOLOv8n running on Raspberry Pi is more accurate and faster in inference than previous CNN and YOLOv5-based models, and hence it is a viable and scalable tool for intelligent transportation infrastructure. The YOLOv8n model (6.2M parameters) achieved an mAP@0.5 of 0.94 and mAP@0.5:0.95 of 0.76.

title: Skill Development Platform with Voice Integration System
authors: A. Deepha, M. Dharsana, S. Fazila, M. Bagya Lakshmi, Y. Harold Robinson
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Intelligent Communication Technologies and Virtual Mobile Networks (ICICV)
abstract: In recent years, due to the rapid growth of online learning platforms, the systems that are capable of assessing and developing users’ skills rather than merely presenting content have been demanded. This report delivers a proposed Skill Development Platform with Voice Integration System that provides aid in interview preparation along with the delivery of online courses. The skill development platform was built using the HTML, CSS and JavaScript technologies to develop a user-friendly web interface; while the backend was developed using Django and a SQLite database. Voice enabled interview simulator is one of the prominent features of the system. The voice enabled interview simulator simulates an interview by using TTS technology to present questions to the user and STT technology to capture and convert the user’s spoken response to text. The AI based evaluation engine analyzes the user’s response to decide a score and provides feedback to the user based on how closely the user’s response associates with the expected response through keyword matching and semantic analysis. Ultimately, the proposed system enhances student success by providing real-time evaluation, interactive feedback via the simulated interviews to increase student confidence and prepare students for the corporate world.

title: The Use of a Scintillation Detector in Equipment for Static Probing During Gamma Logging Tests of Soils
authors: O. V. Biryukova, I. V. Koretskaya
publish: 2026 Wave Electronics and its Application in Information and Telecommunication Systems (WECONF)
abstract: The work is devoted to the development of a radiometer used in equipment for static probing during gamma logging tests of soils in their natural occurrence conditions. The essence of the method consists in recording gamma decay events, the radiation of which hits the scintillation detector. The photomultiplier converts the light flash arising from the illumination of scintillator crystal electrons into an electrical pulse. To conduct gamma logging, it is necessary to ensure the counting of pulses at the photomultiplier output per unit of time. A high-voltage power supply circuit for the photomultiplier was developed using a two-transistor generator with positive feedback. The classical Royer multivibrator circuit was taken as a basis. An analysis of the influence of the element base on the output voltage and stability of the circuit operation was carried out. Circuit modifications were proposed to optimize the characteristics. Prototyping of the power supply was carried out for its operation under field test conditions. A method for calibrating a natural gamma radiation detector has been proposed and tested. It allows establishing a lower triggering threshold and rejecting noise associated with radiation pulses originating outside the control environment. A reference closed spectrometric photon radiation source was used to calibrate the detector. The design of the gamma logging probe (radiometer) combined with a static probe was tested in field conditions as part of the PIKA-19 static probing equipment.

title: Real-Time Posture Correction using MediaPipe
authors: Mohanapriya S, Mohana Saranya S, Dinesh K, Jaya Nirenjan A C, Kavinnath A R, Keshore V M
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Intelligent Communication Technologies and Virtual Mobile Networks (ICICV)
abstract: This project aims to tackle the issue of corrections and lack of repetition in the face of the home exercise without having a human trainer to check the posture of the exercise. Squats, push-ups and bicep curls are some of the exercises that many people carry out poorly, resulting in low effectiveness and possible injuries. The available fitness programs do not necessarily have automatic performance evaluation and real-time corrections of forms. In order to address these constraints, the proposed system would support an intelligent camera-based virtual trainer which could identify the wrong postures, register the repetitions and provide live corrective feedback; thus, facilitating safe and effective self training exercises. It is a Python project utilizing MediaPipe BlazePose to identify 33 body key points parts in live video streams. It measures the joint angles to determine real time movements, categorizes the stages of an exercise and determines accuracy of the form. It has a modular design that is shaped to suit various exercises and skill levels. The Streamlit interface has real-time feedback, automatic tally of rep, posture correct and historical data of daily exercise, and progress tracking. Three main exercises were put to test on this project and they included bicep curls, squats, and pushups. The model is highly accurate and reliable with accurateity of 96% and precision of 97%, recall of 97%, and F1-score of 97% in case of bicep curls. In the case of squats, it achieved a 97% accuracy, 98% precision, 98% recall and F1-score of 98%. With push-ups, it had an accuracy of 96% at 95% precision, a recall of 97% and an F1-score of 96% which means that the performance was slightly worse since the patterns of motion used are more complex. In general, the findings confirm that the system can be used as an efficient AI-powered fitness assistant that should be capable of providing a consistent and precise exercise tracking, which will allow one to adopt scalable, pose-driven fitness analytics.

title: Comparative Implementation and Evaluation of Deep Learning Models for Ultrasound Image Analysis
authors: Prabhneet Singh, Narinder Kaur
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Intelligent Communication Technologies and Virtual Mobile Networks (ICICV)
abstract: Ultrasound (US) imaging is an omnipresent part of clinical practice but insignificant to analyze automatically due to speckle noise, operator-dependency, and low-contrast nature. An implementation-focused review of VGG -19, ResNet -121, Inception, Xception, DenseNet, and Vision Transformers (ViT) in the US image classification and segmentation is presented in the paper. We build up practical understanding about pre-processing, training regimes and compute cost, and compare model performance as denoted by Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-score, ROC -AUC, MAE, MCC, and Cohen K, and contrast model performance with Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1 -score, ROC -AUC, MAE, MCC, and Cohen K on larger or multi-center US cohorts when robust augmentation and pretraining are implemented. As a summary of the results, we conclude with implementation guidelines and indicate gaps in research, such as self-supervision, hybrid CNN-Transformer blocks, and the ability to change domains.

title: ICCSSE 2025 Author Index
authors: N/A
publish: 2025 IEEE 11th International Conference on Control Science and Systems Engineering (ICCSSE)

title: AI-Driven Fault Diagnosis of Induction Motors: A Fault-Type-Based Comparative Review
authors: Rudong Li, Tianru Zhang, Chenyu Lu, Jie Sun
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Electrical Engineering, Control and Robotics (EECR)
abstract: Induction motor (IM) reliability motivates rigorous fault detection and diagnosis (FDD). The task spans multiple fault families, sensing modalities, and operating regimes, which calls for a structured comparison that links faults, signals, and processing pipelines. Therefore, this survey proposes a comparative framework organized by three major fault families: stator interturn short circuits, bearing defects, and rotor faults. It maps each family to informative observables, including stator current, vibration, and infrared thermography (IRT), and to processing workflows that span classical motor current signature analysis (MCSA), time-frequency representations, and artificial intelligence (AI) using machine learning and deep learning. We consolidate evidence from laboratory and public datasets that cover steady, transient, and variable speed or load regimes, and we review representative signal transforms such as the short-time Fourier transform and wavelet methods together with learners including support vector machines, probabilistic neural networks, convolutional neural networks (CNNs), and hybrid models that combine CNNs with long short-term memory (LSTM) units. The synthesis clarifies complementarities between methods and sensors, outlines trade-offs among interpretability, computational cost, and data requirements, and explains how physics-guided data generation and digital twins, combined with transfer learning, address data scarcity and improve generalization across machines. The outcome is a taxonomy and a set of practical guidelines that support lightweight, explainable, and ready for deployment IM FDD across diverse operating conditions.

title: GaN HEMT Transistors Application Features in Outphasing Transmitters
authors: Quoc Fung Ngo, Oleg V. Varlamov
publish: 2026 Wave Electronics and its Application in Information and Telecommunication Systems (WECONF)
abstract: This article examines the GaN HEMT transistors application features in outphasing transmitters built using switching generators direct summation method. A comparative analysis of the third-quadrant current-voltage characteristics of MOSFETs and GaN HEMT transistors is conducted, along with a simulation of their operation in a transmitter circuit. It is shown that despite a larger voltage drop across the off-state transistor in the third quadrant, the use of GaN HEMTs does not introduce additional nonlinearities and provides higher energy efficiency compared to MOSFETs. The obtained results confirm the feasibility of replacing MOSFETs with GaN HEMTs in switching mode RF applications, provided that the gate is correctly driven. This work demonstrates the effectiveness of GaN technology for constructing next-generation, highly efficient, linear RF transmitting devices.

title: Group Priority Indexing Assisted Power Allocation in MIMO-UFMC Systems
authors: Ramachandran Vijayarani, Aishwarya V, Vishnupriyai R, Jenifer Sandier M
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Intelligent Communication Technologies and Virtual Mobile Networks (ICICV)
abstract: In this paper, a GPI-based resource allocation scheme is developed to distribute the resource among multiple user-groups in a fair and balanced manner in wireless communication systems. The GPI based method evaluates a priority index for each group with respect to the system metrics such as channel condition, demand requirement, and fairness constraint, and assigns resources dynamically based on the priorities. By recursing on group priorities and allocations, the framework simultaneously guarantees that higher ranked groups receive the adequate resources. The GPI procedure is terminated when all groups have been allocated and convergence is reached. Experimental results show that the proposed service level aware resource allocation scheme can significantly reduce resource contention, improve priority-based allocation, and achieve a high system utilization under different network scenarios. In addition, the GPI scheme has low computational complexity and can be implemented in real-time for future wireless networks, which can facilitate scalable and adaptive resource management.

title: Enhancing Data Integrity and Authenticity for Blockchain Powered Online Healthcare Services
authors: Anguraju Krishnan, Rajesh Arunachalam
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Intelligent Communication Technologies and Virtual Mobile Networks (ICICV)
abstract: Healthcare fraud in insurance claims imposes substantial financial and operational burdens on healthcare systems, necessitating secure and intelligent detection mechanisms. Existing machine learning and blockchain-based frameworks often lack relational modeling capability, interpretability, or scalable real-time validation within decentralized environments. To address these limitations, this study proposes a Blockchain-Integrated Trust-Aware Graph Attention Learning (TA-GAL) framework for secure and interpretable healthcare fraud detection using the NHIS Healthcare Claims and Fraud Dataset. The model constructs a claim-level relational graph and dynamically adjusts attention weights based on trust propagation, while a permissioned blockchain implemented using Hyperledger Fabric with PBFT consensus ensures tamper-resistant transaction validation. The proposed model was implemented using Python (PyTorch, PyTorch Geometric, and Hyperledger Fabric SDK). Experimental results demonstrate superior performance, achieving 96.84% accuracy, 95.35% F1-score, and 0.982 ROC-AUC, reflecting an approximate 4% improvement over conventional ensemble baselines. The framework effectively enhances transparency, security, and fraud detection reliability, making it suitable for secure, real-world healthcare claim auditing environments.

title: Identifying Comorbid Mental Disorders via Causal Multi-Label KNN
authors: Tiantian Wang, Mengchu Zhou
abstract: Accurate and efficient identification of comorbid mental disorders is a critical challenge in intelligent healthcare. Existing multi-label classification methods often neglect causal dependencies among disorders and between patient features and diagnostic outcomes, which limits their interpretability and clinical reliability. To address this issue, we propose a novel framework called Causal Inference K-Nearest Neighbors (CI-KNN). It integrates causal discovery into the multi-label classification process. Specifically, it introduces a causal feature-label subspace optimization strategy to eliminate spurious correlations and constructs a label-label causal network to capture directional influences among comorbid disorders. Furthermore, a causality-based probability correction mechanism is applied to refine prior and posterior probabilities. We have evaluated the proposed framework on a large-scale psychiatric dataset containing 8,079 clinical cases from the Seventh People’s Hospital in Hangzhou. Experimental results demonstrate that CI-KNN achieves superior performance in terms of accuracy, F1 score, and interpretability compared to state-of-the-art baselines. By bridging causal inference with diagnostic automation, this work provides reliable support for clinical decision-making in mental health assessment.

title: Experimental and Simulation Investigation of Heat Transfer of Large-scale Mini-channel Printed Circuit Heat Exchanger
authors: Haojiang Ran, Zhen Li, Zitian Niu, Zenong Fang, Jianyin Miao
publish: 2025 IEEE 11th International Conference on Control Science and Systems Engineering (ICCSSE)
abstract: This study addresses the challenge of heat dissipation in high-power electric propulsion systems. The heat transfer characteristics of high temperature and high pressure water at low Reynolds numbers in a large-scale semi-circular mini-channel Printed Circuit Heat Exchanger (PCHE) were investigated through simulation and experimentation. Results indicate that the heat transfer process within the mini-channels is significantly influenced by scale effect, with the single-phase flows in the laminar region, Nusselt number remaining constant at $\mathbf{1. 2 8}$. At this channel scale, the Confinement number significantly exceeds 0.5, the surface tension forces play a primary role in this study. Based on the findings, a simplified thermal resistance network model was developed. This model predicted the heat transfer process. Furthermore, an overall heat transfer coefficient correlation was established for the entire process spanning from subcooled water to boiling, providing accurate prediction of the heat transfer coefficient.

title: PDF Knowledge Extraction System with RAG and CAG Models
authors: P. Venkateswarlu Reddy, Nelimela Varalakshmi, Mudadla Keerthana, Mappeti Sri Harsha Tej, Pamula Pranathi
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Intelligent Communication Technologies and Virtual Mobile Networks (ICICV)
abstract: The given project includes a PDF Knowledge Extraction System based on a hybrid model of Retriever-Augmented Generation (RAG) and Cache-Augmented Generation (CAG) models, which are used to optimize querying documents. As the amount of unstructured data increases in PDFs, conventional ways of retrieving information find it difficult to maintain the richness of documents in their context. In the proposed system, two parallel embedding pipelines will be used, the first one will be powered by Google Gemini 1.5 Flash API to generate high-quality embeddings of RAG, and the second will be powered by HuggingFace models to cache CAG. The embeddings are stored in ChromaDB and they are quickly retrieved and generated to respond. Whenever a query is requested, the system looks at the cache to see whether a query will be quickly answered (cache hit) or the query will be processed by RAG and stored in the cache to be used later (cache miss). This will increase the speed, efficiency and scale of the system and hence the system is appropriate in industries like legal research, medical documentation and academic research.

title: Microwave Filter Digital Twin Development
authors: Sergey Vishnyakov, Vadim Lazarev, Yulia Vishnyakova
publish: 2026 8th International Conference on Information Technologies in Engineering Education (Inforino)
abstract: Paper describes two types of models for the digital twin of the dielectric resonator microwave filter. The issues of the dielectric resonator microwave filter design are discussed, including dual-mode and multimodal filters, improved spurious performance filters. The architecture of the filter intellectual adjustment device is proposed. The advantages and disadvantages of lumped element models and numerical FEM/FDTD models are evaluated in relation to the specific issue of adjusting the microwave filter. The paper describes some results of the intellectual control unit design (based on artificial neural networks – multilayer perceptron and Kolmogorov-Arnold network) for the automated microwave filter adjustment.

title: Investigation of Sensor Installation Methods for Acoustic Tomography in Standing Trees
authors: I. V. Matelenok
publish: 2026 Wave Electronics and its Application in Information and Telecommunication Systems (WECONF)
abstract: The paper is devoted to a survey of acoustic sensor installation methods for defect detection in standing trees with stress-wave tomography. Based on an analysis of detachable joint designs used in various technical applications, three potentially suitable attachment methods were selected. Prototype devices for sensor installation on tree trunks were developed. The prototypes were tested within an integrated hardware-software system in experiments using a trunk section of a hardwood tree affected by fungal decay. The electrical signals generated by sensors mounted on the trunk in response to stress waves induced by hitting with impact tools were analyzed. The coupling mount demonstrated the best trade-off between installation speed and reliable detection of the wavefront.

title: RIS-Assisted 6G Link Adaptation with Robust Beamforming Under Channel Uncertainty
authors: CH. Rajeswari, Manikandan I, Mahip Aggarwal, V Sumalatha
publish: 2026 ASU International Conference in Emerging Technologies for Sustainability and Intelligent Systems (ICETSIS)
abstract: Reconfigurable intelligent surfaces (RISs) can reshape radio propagation and are widely viewed as a key enabler for sixth-generation (6 G) coverage and energy efficiency, especially at millimeter-wave and terahertz bands. However, practical RIS deployments suffer from imperfect cascaded channel estimation and fast channel variation, which can cause severe SNR mismatch and unreliable adaptive modulation and coding (AMC). This paper proposes a reliability-oriented link-adaptation framework for RIS-assisted downlink multiple-input single-output (MISO) systems under norm-bounded channel uncertainty. Derive a worst-case SNR expression and obtain a closed-form robust beamformer that maximizes the minimum received SNR subject to a transmit-power constraint. Building on this, design a conservative AMC policy that selects the modulation and coding scheme using the worst-case SNR, thereby guaranteeing the target decoding threshold despite channel errors. Monte-Carlo evaluations show that, for 20 % channel-error bounds, the proposed scheme suppresses throughput outages and maintains stable spectral efficiency compared with nominal SNR-based adaptation, with only a small penalty at moderate SNR.

title: An Intelligent Blockchain Consensus Enhancement Framework Using DAG and the PHANTOM Protocol
authors: Charpe Prasanjeet Prabhakar, Sumit Ramswami Punam, Sayfiddinova Muniskhon Fakhriddinkizi, Geetha R, R. Srinivasan, Al-Rubai Maitham Ali Naji
publish: 2026 ASU International Conference in Emerging Technologies for Sustainability and Intelligent Systems (ICETSIS)
abstract: The blockchain consensus protocols provide trust, security, and consistency of data in decentralized networks. The PHANTOM protocol is a strong consensus mechanism that plays an important role in providing scalable, secure blockchain networks, particularly when using a Directed Acyclic Graph topology. Past blockchain networks are inefficient with low throughput, sequential block confirmation, and high confirmation latency, and are highly limited in scale. To address these problems, the new framework proposes a High-Throughput Cryptocurrency Network that combines the PHANTOM protocol with a Directed Acyclic Graph-based ledger. PHANTOM's non-linear ability to organize blocks enables parallel verification and efficient conflict resolution, and the Directed Acyclic Graph provides structural flexibility and high data transmission speed. The new methodology is used to ensure the reliability of transaction processing in cryptocurrency networks. The experimental results demonstrate higher throughput, a higher block confirmation rate, and stronger consensus guarantees, indicating that the framework has successfully addressed the weaknesses of typical blockchain protocols. The proposed framework improves network stability under high load by reducing bottlenecks caused by linear block propagation and competing miners. The PHANTOM- Directed Acyclic Graph integration enhances equity between the nodes involved in block creation and validation and decreases the likelihood of orphaned blocks due to the ability to create and validate several blocks simultaneously. The architecture is more scalable, as it can add nodes without compromising performance, stability, or security. The strategy provides a useful foundation for next-generation decentralized payment systems and real-time financial applications that require low latency, high throughput, and strong consensus guarantees.

title: PrivacyMotiv: Vulnerability-Centered Persona Journeys for Empathic Privacy Reviews in UX Design
authors: Zeya Chen, Jianing Wen, Yaxing Yao, Toby Jia-Jun Li, Tianshi Li
publish: Proceedings of the 2026 Designing Interactive Systems Conference
abstract: None

title: Machine Learning-based Beam Association for Massive MIMO in Dense Distributed Renewable Energy Networks
authors: Hala Adnan Fadel, Ahmed Al-Dulaimi, Maryam K. Hasan
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Intelligent Communication Technologies and Virtual Mobile Networks (ICICV)
abstract: One of the major challenges facing large-scale MIMO systems is their operation in or near wind farms. These systems suffer from two main problems that significantly affect wave propagation quality: the scattering environment caused by the turbine structure and the interference resulting from the rotating blades. Unlike urban MIMO systems, which have received considerable research, the phenomena specific to the operation of these systems in remote areas and wind farms require specialized processing techniques. In this paper, a geo-contextual framework is proposed a geo-contextual framework for the K-Nearest Neighbor (KNN) algorithm to select the optimal beam from a five-dimensional feature vector: the user device (UE) coordinates projected onto the ENU, the average received signal strength, and the generated Wind Speed Interference Index (WSII). This approach thus eliminating the need for experimental surveys during inference. Data from 12 wind farm sites spanning six months were used and trained using KNN techniques (K = 7, determined by ten-fold cross-validation). The proposed model achieved 98.7% selection accuracy (F1-score 0.971, across 16 classes) with an inference response time of 2.1 ms, outperforming both the SVM baseline (91.4%, 3.8 ms) and the CNN fingerprint approach (96.8%, 11.2 ms). The cost of experimental signals was reduced from O(M) = 16 bits per user request to O(1), resulting in approximately 1600 pairs of experimental signals and bits per coherence interval when serving 100 users simultaneously. Evaluation on reserved sites not used during training demonstrated 89.0% accuracy, providing evidence of practical portability between sites.

title: Automatic Adaptation of Mutation Rates for Move Acceptance Hyper-Heuristics Using Hypermutations
authors: Pietro S. Oliveto, Mengqing Xu
abstract: Move Acceptance Hyper-Heuristics (MAHHs) switch probabilistically between elitist and non-elitist acceptance rules to escape local optima. Previous work from the literature has shown that if a MAHH is equipped with a one-bit mutation operator (MAHH1-bit), then it remarkably optimizes the multimodal CLIFF function class much faster than the best possible elitist evolutionary algorithms (EAs) and non-elitist algorithms such as Metropolis. However, on harder to escape function classes such as standard JUMP functions, its performance is considerably worse than that of elitist EAs. If the MAHH framework is extended to use global mutation operators that allow for larger step-sizes than just one-bit flips, then its performance for JUMP becomes at least as good as that of elitist EAs, but the algorithm loses its advantages for CLIFF. Since high mutation rates are beneficial for JUMP-like functions and low ones instead for CLIFF-like functions, we propose a Fast Adaptive MAHH𝛽, which integrates a power-law hypermutation operator with a self-adaptive mechanism to dynamically adjust the mutation rate. We rigorously prove that the algorithm achieves remarkably fast convergence on both CLIFF and JUMP functions, while maintaining optimal asymptotic performance on the ONEMAX unimodal function.

title: Self-Supervised Seismic Data Reconstruction: A Novel Network with GA-Optimized Jittered Sampling for Improving Crossline Spatial Sparsity
authors: Run Jiang, Shaoping Lu, Kai Ren, Guanghui Han, Xiao Ge
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: In marine seismic exploration, towed-streamer seismic data acquisition is a critical strategy, yet it often faces significant challenges due to sparse spatial sampling along the crossline direction. This limitation hampers seismic imaging quality, particularly in multiple wave imaging and the resolution of fine subsurface structures. While Fourier interpolation is commonly used to mitigate these issues, traditional methods struggle with the large crossline spacing and limited cable numbers in narrow-azimuth marine towed-streamer surveys. The advent of deep learning offers a promising solution to these challenges. Therefore, we propose a novel self-supervised learning approach to enhance crossline spatial sampling for towed-streamer seismic data. Our method combines a Partial Convolutional Neural Network with a Transformer architecture and incorporates a jittered sampling algorithm optimized by a genetic algorithm. This innovative integration enables the model to effectively capture latent patterns and extract implicit supervisory information from unlabeled seismic data, overcoming the limitations of traditional interpolation techniques. The proposed method was rigorously validated using both a 3D salt model and field data. Results demonstrate its ability to significantly improve crossline spatial sampling, leading to enhanced seismic imaging resolution and accuracy. This advancement not only addresses the inherent challenges of towed-streamer surveys but also opens new possibilities for high-quality subsurface characterization in marine seismic exploration.

title: Evaluating Satellite and Model Sea Surface Salinity with In Situ Observation from an Autonomous Surface Vehicle
authors: Vehicle Samuel M. Ayim, Leonie Jaeger, Lisa Gassen, Oliver Wurl
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Accurate estimation of sea surface salinity (SSS) in coastal regions remains a major challenge because of intense sub-mesoscale variability, radio frequency interference, and land contamination affecting satellite retrievals. This study evaluates seven SSS products. The products include two single-mission satellite products (SMOS and SMAP), three multiple-mission satellite products (CNR, CATDS and ESA CCI), one model reanalysis product (GLORYSS), and ship-based thermosalinograph (TSG) data against high-resolution in situ observations collected by the autonomous surface vehicle (ASV) HALOBATES. The ASV measured skin salinity at a sub-kilometre scale, providing a dataset to assess both satellite and reanalysis products in a highly dynamic environment. Validation metrics, including correlation, bias, mean absolute error (MAE), root mean square deviation (RMSD), unbiased RMSD (ubRMSD), and median absolute deviation (MAD), were used to quantify product performance. The results show that the TSG exhibited the strongest consistency with HALOBATES observations (r = 0.98; RMSD = 0.24 g/kg), confirming the robustness of co-located in situ systems. GLORYSS performed moderately with r = 0.59 and RMSD = 0.96 g/kg. SMOS (r = 0.67) and SMAP (r = 0.64) yielded comparable correlations but RMSD of 1.04 and 1.11 g/kg respectively, while the merged products CNR (r = 0.35), CATDS (r = 0.55) and ESA CCI (r = 0.32) exhibited lower correlations and higher RMSD of 1.11, 1.34 and 1.36 g/kg respectively. Due to vertical stratification, we observed that not using in situ skin salinity in the validation process can create biases. Overall, satellite-based SSS retrievals were most sensitive to spatial collocation distance, confirming that unresolved coastal gradients and footprint heterogeneity are the key error drivers.

title: High-Gain Hydrogen-Terminated Diamond Logic Circuits with Low power-consumption
authors: Lei Ge, Siying Gao, Xuemin Zhao, Mingyang Wang, Yaxin Li, Runlei Zhang, Xiaohua Chen, Saibin Han, Yingnan Wang, Yan Peng, Zai-xing Yang, Jisheng Han, Mingsheng Xu, Xiangang Xu
abstract: Hydrogen-terminated diamond field effect transistors (FETs) are typically depletion-mode devices, which limits their application in low-power-consumption logic circuits. Here, high-performance inverters and ring oscillators have been demonstrated by using normally-off Al/BaF2/H-diamond FETs. The adoption of enhancement-mode transistors enables the realization of low-static-power logic circuits by employing diamond FETs with different channel lengths as load and driver devices. The peak gain of the inverter increases from −100 V/V to −460 V/V as the supply voltage ranges from −1 V to −5 V with a static power consumption low to ~1 pW, marking the highest gain reported for diamond-based inverters. Furthermore, a five-stage ring oscillator operates at 58.8 kHz with a stage delay of 1.7 μs under a supply voltage of −3 V, marking the first successful implementation of a diamond-based ring oscillator. These results highlight the great potential of diamond technology for high-speed power integrated circuit applications.

title: Table of Contents
authors: N/A

title: Editorial: TSC Celebrates IEEE Computer Society's 80th Anniversary
authors: Liang-Jie Zhang, Ling Liu, James B.D. Joshi, Ernesto Damiani, Surya Nepal, Marco Aiello

title: Curriculum Domain Adaptation via Wavelet Mixup for Cyber–Physical–Social Intelligence
authors: Xuejun Cheng, Haiteng Wang, Zidi Jia, Lei Ren
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computational Social Systems
abstract: Cyber–physical–social intelligence (CPSI) requires robust perception models that generalize across heterogeneous human-centered sensing environments. However, sensor data in cyber–physical–social systems (CPSSs) often exhibit strong nonstationarity, low signal-to-noise ratios, and substantial cross-subject distribution shifts, which can severely degrade model performance. Existing adversarial unsupervised domain adaptation (UDA) methods mainly focus on feature-level alignment, but they often underuse target-domain structural information when training the task classifier, especially under large source–target discrepancies. To address this issue, we propose wavelet-enhanced curriculum domain adaptation (Wave-CDA), a curriculum-based UDA framework with wavelet-domain mixup for CPSS-relevant sensor time series. Wave-CDA consists of three stages: 1) adversarial UDA pretraining for learning transferable representations; 2) multilevel wavelet mixup (WMix) for generating intermediate-domain samples that preserve important time–frequency structures while incorporating unlabeled target information; and 3) transferability-guided curriculum training that progressively introduces augmented samples from easier-to-transfer samples to harder samples. We further design three transferability measures, namely label confidence, label agreement, and domain confusion, to assess the reliability and usefulness of augmented samples. Experiments on cross-subject emotion recognition, human activity recognition, and human gesture recognition show that Wave-CDA consistently outperforms competitive baselines. Additional analyses of ablation, transferability weighting, and training behavior further support the effectiveness of wavelet-domain augmentation and curriculum scheduling.

title: Hybrid CIR/ToA UWB Localization via Fingerprint Interval Prediction and GMM-PSO Optimization
authors: Shixun Wu, Sen Teng, Zhongwei Hou, Kai Xu, Zhangli Lan, Miao Zhang, Kanapathippillai Cumanan
abstract: To mitigate the significant degradation of Ultra-Wideband (UWB) positioning accuracy in non-line-of-sight (NLoS) scenarios, this paper proposes an integrated framework that synergizes Channel Impulse Response (CIR) fingerprinting with Time of Arrival (ToA) ranging. Unlike conventional methods that treat fingerprinting and ranging independently, we introduce a novel uncertainty-aware fusion strategy. Specifically, an attention-enhanced Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) is first employed to extract environmental features from CIR data. Crucially, instead of direct coordinate regression, we utilize a Copula-based conformal prediction(CP) method to quantify the positioning uncertainty and construct a reliable spatial search constraint. This constraint then guides a Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) algorithm to robustly identify the optimal position within the predicted region when ToA ranging errors are modeled by a Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM). This mechanism effectively filters out large outliers by leveraging the complementary strengths of feature matching and geometric ranging. Experimental results on public datasets demonstrate that the proposed method achieves a mean error of 0.0911 meters, outperforming six state-of-the-art algorithms by at least 8.35% while satisfying high computational efficiency requirements.

title: Eddy Current Loss-Oriented Design of an Axial-Flux PM Machine Using Improved Rotor Bracket and Partially Segmented PM Design
authors: Zhiguo Zhu, Xiping Liu, Chen Wang, Dabin Liu, Zhangqi Liu, Dong Chen, Qianli Jia
abstract: Eddy current loss (ECL) critically affects the thermal performance of double-stator single-rotor axial flux permanent magnet machines (AFPMMs). In particular, ECL in the rotor bracket is often overlooked, leading to underestimated heating and inaccurate thermal analysis. To address this issue, this paper presents an ECL-oriented design methodology, incorporating both the improved rotor bracket and partially segmented PM design to collaboratively reduce ECL. First, analytical models of ECL in both rotor bracket and PM are established, providing design guidelines for effective ECL reduction. Based on these guidelines, the groove and groove-segmentation rotor bracket designs along with a partially segmented PM structure, are proposed and introduced. Then, three-dimensional (3D) finite element (FE) analyses are conducted on the proposed designs to evaluate ECL and electromagnetic performance relative to the baseline. The improved rotor brackets achieve at least a 38.5% reduction in ECL, while the partially segmented PM structure yields a 37% reduction, with negligible degradation in electromagnetic performance. Furthermore, structural and thermal analyses are conducted to assess mechanical integrity and thermal characteristics. Finally, an AFPMM prototype incorporating the improved rotor bracket and partially segmented PM design is manufactured and experimentally tested, validating the effectiveness and feasibility of the machine design.

title: A Distributed Multiagent Wireless Federated Learning Algorithm with Local Differential Privacy
authors: Jing Wang, Steven Drager
abstract: Federated Learning (FL) has emerged as a promising framework for privacy-preserving distributed learning by enabling model training across multiple agents (clients) without exchanging raw data. However, FL remains vulnerable to inference attacks and faces challenges in scalability, robustness, and efficiency, especially in wireless and decentralized environments. In this paper, we propose a novel distributed multiagent wireless federated learning algorithm with client-level local differential privacy, where agents communicate over wireless channels and aggregate model updates using wireless channel-aware computation. By explicitly modeling the wireless channel and incorporating artificial noise, each agent performs a consensus-based gradient estimation using its own and its neighbors’ updates. We rigorously prove that under a strongly connected topology and suitable learning-rate conditions, convergence is guaranteed. Client-level differential privacy is further ensured via power control at the channel level. Our approach offers a robust and scalable solution for mission-critical applications such as healthcare and autonomous driving in adversarial environments. Comprehensive simulation results validate the theoretical convergence properties, analyze the privacy-utility tradeoff, and empirically validate privacy protection against membership inference attacks. Results confirm that the distributed algorithm achieves performance comparable to centralized federated learning while providing stronger privacy guarantees and improved fault tolerance.

title: Scalable Quantum Message Passing Graph Neural Networks for Next-Generation Wireless Communications: Architectures, Use Cases, and Future Directions
authors: Le Tung Giang, Nguyen Xuan Tung, Trinh Van Chien, Lajos Hanzo, Won-Joo Hwang
abstract: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) are eminently suitable for wireless resource management, thanks to their scalability, but they still face computational challenges in large-scale, dense networks on classical computers. The integration of quantum computing with GNNs offers a promising pathway for enhancing computational efficiency, because they may reduce the model complexity. This is achieved by leveraging the quantum advantages of parameterized quantum circuits (PQCs), while retaining the expressive power of GNNs. However, existing pure quantum message passing models remain constrained by the limited number of qubits, hence limiting the scalability of their application to wireless systems. As a remedy, we conceive a Scalable Quantum Message Passing Graph Neural Network (SQM-GNN) relying on a quantum message passing architecture. To address the aforementioned scalability issue, we decompose the graph into subgraphs and apply a shared PQC to each local subgraph. Importantly, the model incorporates both node and edge features, facilitating the full representation of the underlying wireless graph structure. We demonstrate the efficiency of SQM-GNN on a device-to-device (D2D) power control task, where it outperforms both classical GNNs and heuristic baselines. These results highlight SQM-GNN as a promising direction for future wireless network optimization.

title: Deep Learning Approach for Microwave Imaging Based on Deep Convolutional Asymmetric Encoder-Decoder Structure and Physics-Induced Loss
authors: He Ming Yao, Shiji Song, Michael Ng, Lijun Jiang
abstract: In this paper, we introduce an innovative deep learning (DL) methodology designed for real-time quantitative microwave imaging (MWI). Our approach is centered around the utilization of a deep convolutional asymmetric encoder-decoder structure (DCAEDS), which requires only a single-frequency far-field measurement of the electromagnetic (EM) scattered field as input and subsequently predicts the contrasts (permittivities) of the target materials. During the offline training process, we incorporate an EM forward solver specifically crafted to compute the EM scattered field generated by the predicted target contrasts (permittivities) produced by the DCAEDS. The DCAEDS is seamlessly integrated with this EM forward solver to optimize the loss function. This loss function comprises two fundamental components: (1) Data-induced loss, directly quantifying the dissimilarity between the predictions of our proposed DCAEDS and the actual labels for the target contrasts (permittivities); (2) Physics-induced loss, evaluating the distinctions between the measured EM scattered field and the computed EM scattered field derived from the predicted target contrasts (permittivities) generated by the DCAEDS. Our DL approach excels in delivering precise results, even for high-contrast targets, overcoming limitations associated with conventional methods, such as computational cost and ill-conditioning. Numerical benchmarks using dielectric targets underscore the practicality and effectiveness of our DL-based approach.

title: One to Rule Them All: Reinforcement Learning Policy Selection with Closed-Loop Sensitivity
authors: Andrea Pupa, Alberto Dionigi, Paolo Robuffo Giordano, Cristian Secchi, Gabriele Costante
abstract: In reinforcement learning-based control, reliable zero-shot transfer from simulation to real hardware remains a major open challenge. The offline training phase typically produces multiple models, each corresponding to a different policy, and choosing which model to deploy on physical hardware is a critical decision. However, there is no established methodology for this selection process. A common practice is to deploy the policy that achieves the highest reward in simulation, without evaluating its robustness to uncertainties. As a result, due to the sim-to-real gap, the selected policy may perform poorly when transferred to a physical robotic system, potentially leading to unsafe or undesirable behavior. This work proposes to exploit the notion of “closed-loop sensitivity” for applying it, for the first time, to the analysis and comparison of different reinforcement learning models, i.e., different policies, and to estimate their expected performance on real hardware. The proposed metric captures how the closed-loop system reacts to modeling errors, providing a quantitative measure of policy robustness. Both simulation results and real world experimental campaigns demonstrate that the proposed approach enables reliable zero-shot transfer to a real robot, while effectively handling uncertainties and reducing the risk of unsafe behaviors.

title: A High-Reliability Gas-Sensor-Based PUF for Secure IoT
authors: Zhanbo Chen, Shanshan Wang, Xiaowei Zhang, Hao Ye, Pengjun Wang, Xiangyu Li
abstract: This work introduces a Physical Unclonable Function (PUF) architecture based on WO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2.90</sub> gas sensors, generating cryptographic keys directly from the microscopic structural randomness of nanomaterials. We propose a multi-dimensional resistive arbitration algorithm to dynamically map the sensors' inherent resistive variations into a scalable challenge-response space. Systematic experimental evaluations demonstrate an ideal uniqueness of 50.02% and an exceptionally low average Bit Error Rate (BER) of 1.45%. Furthermore, the PUF maintains high operational robustness across varying voltages, temperatures, and time drift. To validate its cryptographic rigor, a comprehensive dataset of 10,000 challenge-response pairs (CRPs) was evaluated. The results confirm that the generated bitstreams successfully pass the NIST SP 800-22 statistical test suite, yielding an effective min-entropy of 127.84 bits for 128-bit responses. Additionally, the architecture exhibits strong resilience against classic machine learning modeling attacks. These physical and cryptographic validations establish the proposed gas-sensor PUF as a highly secure, scalable, and reliable hardware primitive.

title: Spectral Graph Theory: The mathematics of self-supervised learning [Special Issue on the Mathematics of Deep Learning]
authors: Randall Balestriero, Yann LeCun
abstract: Possessing a manipulable representation of the world is a requirement for intelligent machines to plan, reason, and act in the world. Endowing computational systems, e.g., deep networks (DNs), with artificial intelligence (AI) capabilities is the goal of self-supervised learning (SSL). Immense optimism, fueled by early successes, funneled vast resources into SSL, which led to fast-paced but fragmented early developments. As a result, today’s SSL research has become costly, slow-paced, and often akin to trial and error. To ensure that SSL research remains innovative and fail-proof, we present a unifying mathematical language that underpins SSL as a whole, and from which it is possible to explain and provably alleviate some of its limitations. To that end, we bring forward a spectral graph theory of SSL that will rely on harmonic analysis and spectral graph theory. Under that view, SSL can be summarized as 1) estimating a relationship graph between the dataset samples and 2) learning to produce a smooth signal (from a DN) on that graph, as measured by the Dirichlet energy. The vast collection of SSL methods fall under that formulation and differ only in how they estimate the graph, or how they measure the energy of the prediction on that graph. As part of this development, we also ground SSL to an ideal supervised learning task: SSL objectives minimize the expected risk over all possible downstream tasks. Finally, the practicality of our formulation is showcased by precisely pinpointing the benefits and pitfalls of each method, along with their underlying assumptions on the data and system design. For example, the impact of the data quality and sampling is studied, along with the role of various hyperparameters such as minibatch size, regularization, and data augmentation.

title: Global and Local Visual-Textual Alignment for Open Vocabulary Object Detection
authors: Hao Wang, Tong Jia, Shizhuo Deng, Dongyue Chen, Qilong Wang, Wangmeng Zuo
abstract: Recently, with the development of the Vision-Language Model (VLM), adopting such VLM (e.g., CLIP) into object detection framework has gradually become a promising and attractive research direction, and the resulted open vocabulary object detection methods can effectively alleviate the limitations in those close-set ones, making the detectors perceive the unseen world. The core issue in open vocabulary object detection is to design an effective and efficient alignment between the visual (e.g., image) and textual (e.g., caption) features in the semantic space, so that the detectors can capture more information around the open-set scene. Current approaches deploy extra uncurated image-text pairs to pre-train a detector for obtaining a better visual-textual alignment in the feature space. Besides, knowledge distillation technology is also adopted to design an appropriate information transferring flow for aligning the visual-textual knowledge. However, large-scale image-text pairs are not always available to obtain, and the pretraining process will inevitable introduce much more computation overhead. While knowledge distillation methods focus on aligning between the local region visual feature in RoI and the textual features of VLM, neglecting the global information alignment between the image and text. For addressing the dilemmas in these alignment manners, we propose a Global and Local Visual-Textual Alignment for Open Vocabulary Object Detection in this paper. Specifically, our proposed method integrates global image-caption and local region-prompt alignments into a unified learning paradigm. The global alignment takes the whole image and caption as the visual and textual inputs, respectively, and matches the image and caption representations from the detector and the text encoder in CLIP by contrastive learning from the overall perspective. Different from global alignment, the local one concentrates on the accordance between regions and prompts from the aspect of portion description. It extracts and aligns the embeddings for the visual patch RoIs from the image encoder in CLIP and discriminating textual token prompts from the text encoder. Moreover, we also design a prompt tuning strategy, which contains global and local components corresponding to the alignment procedure, for better adapting CLIP to downstream task object detection in a parameter-efficient learning manner. By implementation on Faster R-CNN, we conduct experiments on open vocabulary benchmarks OV-COCO and OV-LVIS, respectively. The results verify that our proposed method can achieve clear improvement over counterparts on novel categories, while performing favorably against state-of-the-arts.

title: Generalized Power Flow Model of an Extra High-Power Multi-Terminal HVdc Transmission Grid with Parallel-Connected and Voltage-Stacked Converters
authors: Quan Nguyen, Sreenivasa Jaldanki, Suman Debnath, Jinho Kim, Michael M. Abdelmalak, Sameer Nekkalapu, Bharat Vyakaranam, Marcelo Elizondo
publish: IEEE Open Access Journal of Power and Energy
abstract: To increase power transfer capacity of high-voltage direct current (HVdc) transmission, a new extra high-power HVdc architecture with multiple standard modular multilevel converters (MMCs) per substation has recently been introduced. This paper proposes a power flow model for a multi-terminal HVdc grid with this innovative substation architecture. The proposed multi-terminal HVdc power flow model can be integrated seamlessly with existing ac-dc power flow algorithms with minimal modifications. The model is applicable to various multi-terminal HVdc grid types and topologies, different numbers of dc buses, dc lines, and MMCs per substation, along with diverse control parameters. In addition, it accurately captures both balanced and unbalanced operations of the multi-terminal HVdc grid. The proposed model is verified using a 5-terminal bipole HVdc grid that spans 4 areas in the Eastern Interconnection system of the USA. The numerical solutions obtained from unified and sequential ac-dc power flow algorithms under different operating conditions closely match the time-domain simulation results in PSCAD, validating the accuracy and versatility of the proposed multi-terminal HVdc power flow model.

title: Explainable Action Form Assessment by Exploiting Multimodal Chain-of-Thoughts Reasoning
authors: Mengshi Qi, Yeteng Wu, Wulian Yun, Xianlin Zhang, Huadong Ma
abstract: Evaluating whether human action is standard or not and providing reasonable feedback to improve action standardization is very crucial but challenging in real-world scenarios. However, current video understanding methods are mainly concerned with what and where the action is, which is unable to meet the requirements. Meanwhile, most of the existing datasets lack the labels indicating the degree of action standardization, and the action quality assessment datasets lack explainability and detailed feedback. Therefore, we define a new Human Action Form Assessment (AFA) task, and introduce a new diverse dataset CoT-AFA, which contains a large scale of fitness and martial arts videos with multi-level annotations for comprehensive video analysis. We enrich the CoT-AFA dataset with a novel Chain-of-Thought explanation paradigm. Instead of offering isolated feedback, our explanations provide a complete reasoning process—from identifying an action step to analyzing its outcome and proposing a concrete solution. Furthermore, we propose a framework named Explainable Fitness Assessor, which can not only judge an action but also explain why and provide a solution. This framework employs two parallel processing streams and a dynamic gating mechanism to fuse visual and semantic information, thereby boosting its analytical capabilities. The experimental results demonstrate that our method has achieved improvements in explanation generation (e.g., +16.0% in CIDEr) ,action classification (+2.7% in accuracy) and quality assessment (+2.1% in accuracy), revealing great potential of CoT-AFA for future studies. Our dataset and source code is available at https://github.com/MICLAB-BUPT/EFA.

title: A Wired-Logic and Near-Memory Hybrid Processor for General-Purpose Audio Inference Using a Self-Supervised Encoder
authors: Jaewon Shin, Kenji Kobayashi, Kota Shiba, Jiawei Yu, Koji Nii, Tsung-Yung Jonathan Chang, Mototsugu Hamada, Atsutake Kosuge
publish: IEEE Open Journal of the Solid-State Circuits Society
abstract: This work presents a wired-logic (WL) and near-memory (NM) hybrid processor that enables general-purpose audio inference by embedding a compact self-supervised learning (SSL) pretrained encoder into an area-efficient WL core while retaining task-level adaptability through an NM-based classifier. To obtain a compact encoder suitable for WL implementation, we develop an NNN-SSL framework that trains a nonlinear neural network (NNN) using ternary weights, 80% pruning, and 14-bit activations within an augmentation-based SSL pipeline. The resulting 64-channel encoder provides unified feature extraction across speech, environmental sound, and musical-instrument domains. A global-pooling block and a frequency-serial fully connected (FC) architecture support variable-length audio inputs and reduce the FC area from 3.56mm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> to 0.76mm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>. A 40 nm test chip achieves 48.1 TOPS/W for the convolution layer, while the full-network energy is estimated at 0.87 μJ/inference on GSCD-12. Furthermore, scaling the SSL pretraining dataset and the number of epochs enables accuracy improvements under a fixed-area constraint, showing that SSL can compensate for limited model capacity in WL-based designs. By combining SSL-driven general-purpose feature learning with WL-centric energy efficiency and minimal NM programmability, the proposed processor demonstrates a practical path toward compact, low-power, multi-domain audio AI for edge devices.

title: HE-FR: A High-Efficiency Fault-Resilient Framework for Stuck-at-Faults Mitigation in ReRAM-Based In-Memory Computing
authors: Junlong Lin, Wenhua Wu, Mingtao Wang, Wang Kang, Guojun Han
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems I: Regular Papers
abstract: ReRAM-based in-memory computing (IMC) architectures enable efficient neural-network inference on edge devices, due to their high density and non-volatility. However, ReRAM is prone to the stuck-at faults (SAFs) which would distort weight mappings and reduce model accuracy substantially. To tackle this problem, a high-efficiency fault-resilient (HE-FR) framework is introduced in the paper, which couples layered-precision quantization based on the generalized gaussian distribution cumulative distribution function (GGD-CDF), preserving weight distributions with the minimal information loss. Further, a fault-aware weight re-decomposition method is proposed. Specifically, a Q-agent-based method is proposed for sparse SAFs, which employs offline reinforcement learning to construct a globally optimized hash table with reduced compilation complexity, while incorporating both weight matching and cell-state stability into the reward function to suppress electro-stress. For dense SAFs, we exploit a greedy search over the remaining cells to provide rapid and accurate mappings. Experimental results show that GGD-CDF quantization improves accuracy by 2.18% over Float32. Under fabrication faults, HE-FR surpasses a fault-free method by 12.97% and accelerates compilation by <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$197\times $</tex-math> </inline-formula> with only 0.024% picojoule-level energy overhead. In electro-stress evaluations, HE-FR reduces performance degradation by 46.27%. It demonstrates that the proposed framework has advantages in robustness, efficiency, and reliability.

title: A novel switched systems approach to nonconvex optimisation
authors: Joel Ferguson, Saeed Ahmed, Juan E. Machado, Michele Cucuzzella, Jacquelien M. A. Scherpen
abstract: We develop a novel switching dynamics that converges to the Karush-Kuhn-Tucker (KKT) point of a nonlinear optimisation problem. This new approach is particularly notable for its lower dimensionality compared to conventional primal-dual dynamics, as it focuses exclusively on estimating the primal variable. Our method is successfully illustrated on general quadratic optimisation problems, the minimisation of the classical Rosenbrock function, and a nonconvex optimisation problem stemming from the control of energy-efficient buildings.

title: Can Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning-Based Tools Perform Peer Reviews? Perspectives on Possible Framework and Limitations
authors: M. Abul Masrur
abstract: With artificial intelligence- (AI) and machine learning (ML)-based tools being discussed nowadays in academia, industry, and in the media, interesting questions also arise regarding their applications. The title of this article expresses that question. One reason for writing this forward-looking perspective/position article is because this author served in various editorial roles in certain publications, and knowing the challenges in finding reviewer support, this author believes that it will be highly beneficial if an AI/ML-based tool could be utilized for such review support. The scope of this article does not intend to provide demonstration of such a tool, since the development of such a tool will need significant collaboration between multiple entities involving academia, libraries, publishers, and industry. The article also discusses the huge energy consumption of AI/ML-based tools.

title: An Intelligent Gesture-Based Assistive Framework for Smart Presentations and Human-Computer Interaction
authors: Shital Dongre, Ayesha Pathan, Parishnav Thokal, Aayusha Marne, Vedant Motale
publish: 2026 ASU International Conference in Emerging Technologies for Sustainability and Intelligent Systems (ICETSIS)
abstract: A smart gesture-based assistive platform to improve smart presentations and human-computer interface with physically challenged users. The proposed system offers an artificial intelligence-based and touchless presentation control system which allows controlling the slides as well as zooming images on the screen and writing annotations on the screen in real-time with the help of hand-gestures recognition. A streamlined MediaPipe-OpenCV system can be used to achieve a powerful hand tracking and adaptive gesture filtering and misclassification correction can make the system more reliable in the face of diverse user actions and environment parameters. The structure exhibits good adaptability to the environment and it has a high recognition performance in the case of low-light conditions and partial hand covering. Experimental analysis gives an average accuracy of 95, which is higher than baseline gesture recognition methods in all the conditions evaluated. The work offers a scaleable, user-friendly interface that promotes assistive technology and further develops practical gesture-driven interaction among educators, presenters, and other people with motor deficiencies, thus facilitating the creation of more inclusive digital interactions. With the integration of MediaPipe and OpenCV, gesture recognition is on the rise, and it is opening up the possibilities in the field of digital education, interaction, and assistive technologies. It is this inherent aspect of consideration of multi-user adjustability and the expansive variety of gestures that will form the basis of practicality and the expected large variety of applications in education, business, and artistic contexts. The findings suggest that the system offers a non-discriminatory, friendly, and interactive modality of interaction that minimizes reliance on hardware.

title: STEAM Technologies in Computational Geometry
authors: Valery F. Ochkov, Anton I. Tikhonov
publish: 2026 8th International Conference on Information Technologies in Engineering Education (Inforino)
abstract: This article is devoted to a multipurpose story on computational geometry, constructed using STEM and STEAM technologies. Bézier curves are used as the basic geometric primitive. Working with them is mastered using library functions and an interactive editor. Various approaches to the construction complex geometric structures are considered, including L-systems and methods of representing geometric data for linear and nonlinear transformations. Penrose tiling are constructed on a rectangular area and on a disk. A detailed analysis of the design of M.C. Escher Square Limit and Circle Limit for various generating figures is provided. In conclusion, the methodology for applying the story in practical classes at a technical university is discussed.
